The doubled character '好' (good) carried an ironic edge, suggesting performance rather than genuine virtue. Modern usage describes those who avoid all conflict through universal agreeability, even when principled disagreement would better serve the common good.
chineseExample_tr: 這位經理避免必要的批評，從不挑戰糟糕的表現

example: The general secured supply lines to prevent vulnerability during the advance
chineseExample: 将军确保补给线，以防进军过程中出现脆弱性
description: Ancient military strategists coined this phrase about worries (忧) from looking back (后顾), emphasizing how unaddressed vulnerabilities can paralyze forward progress. Han Dynasty political writers expanded it beyond warfare to describe any situation where background concerns prevent bold action. The psychological insight remains relevant - true confidence requires securing one's rear position.
description_tr: Ancient military strategists coined this phrase about worries (憂) from looking back (後顧), emphasizing how unaddressed vulnerabilities can paralyze forward progress. Han Dynasty political writers expanded it beyond warfare to describe any situation where background concerns prevent bold action. The psychological insight remains relevant - true confidence requires securing one's rear position.
chineseExample_tr: 將軍確保補給線，以防進軍過程中出現脆弱性

example: The ancient fortress commanded the mountain pass with intimidating strategic position
chineseExample: 这座古堡以令人生畏的战略位置控制着山口
description: Three Kingdoms period geographers first described Nanjing's terrain as having tigers crouching (虎踞) and dragons coiling (龙盘). The image combined earth's greatest predator with its supreme mythical creature to capture the city's naturally commanding position. Tang Dynasty writers expanded it to describe any position of overwhelming natural advantage. Modern strategic thinking still values such inherent positional strength.
description_tr: Three Kingdoms period geographers first described Nanjing's terrain as having tigers crouching (虎踞) and dragons coiling (龍盤). The image combined earth's greatest predator with its supreme mythical creature to capture the city's naturally commanding position. Tang Dynasty writers expanded it to describe any position of overwhelming natural advantage. Modern strategic thinking still values such inherent positional strength.
chineseExample_tr: 這座古堡以令人生畏的戰略位置控制着山口

example: The conspiracy theorist's explanation lacked any factual foundation
chineseExample: 阴谋论者的解释毫无事实依据
description: Ming Dynasty vernacular speech gave us this colorful description of reckless speaking (胡说) scattered in eight directions (八道). Qing Dynasty scholars used it to criticize statements completely disconnected from evidence or logic. The image of words scattered in all directions perfectly captured the chaos of unfounded assertions. Modern usage spans from conspiracy theories to any confidently stated nonsense.
metaphoric_meaning: Talk complete nonsense without any foundation
description_tr: Ming Dynasty vernacular speech gave us this colorful description of reckless speaking (胡說) scattered in eight directions (八道). Qing Dynasty scholars used it to criticize statements completely disconnected from evidence or logic. The image of words scattered in all directions perfectly captured the chaos of unfounded assertions. Modern usage spans from conspiracy theories to any confidently stated nonsense.
chineseExample_tr: 陰謀論者的解釋毫無事實依據

example: The novel began with dramatic intensity but ended with a weak, rushed conclusion
chineseExample: 这部小说开头激烈，但以弱且仓促的结尾收场
description: Tang Dynasty literary critics gave us this image of a tiger's head (虎头) with a snake's tail (蛇尾) to describe works that begin powerfully but end weakly. Song Dynasty writers expanded it beyond literature to any project showing declining quality over time. The zoological mismatch perfectly captured the disappointment of strong starts fading into weak finishes.
description_tr: Tang Dynasty literary critics gave us this image of a tiger's head (虎頭) with a snake's tail (蛇尾) to describe works that begin powerfully but end weakly. Song Dynasty writers expanded it beyond literature to any project showing declining quality over time. The zoological mismatch perfectly captured the disappointment of strong starts fading into weak finishes.
chineseExample_tr: 這部小說開頭激烈，但以弱且倉促的結尾收場

example: After struggling with the problem for days, the solution suddenly became clear
chineseExample: 经过几天的努力，解决方案突然变得清晰
description: Buddhist meditation traditions gave us this description of sudden clarity (恍然) leading to great awakening (大悟). Unlike gradual enlightenment, this represented those electric moments of instant comprehension. Tang Dynasty writers adopted it for any profound realization after confusion. Today it captures those 'eureka' moments when previously incomprehensible patterns suddenly snap into perfect clarity.
description_tr: Buddhist meditation traditions gave us this description of sudden clarity (恍然) leading to great awakening (大悟). Unlike gradual enlightenment, this represented those electric moments of instant comprehension. Tang Dynasty writers adopted it for any profound realization after confusion. Today it captures those 'eureka' moments when previously incomprehensible patterns suddenly snap into perfect clarity.
chineseExample_tr: 經過幾天的努力，解決方案突然變得清晰

example: The family never mentioned the scandal, treating it as a forbidden topic
chineseExample: 家人从不提及这一丑闻，将其视为禁忌话题
description: Han Dynasty court protocols first established this practice of avoiding discussion (讳莫) as deeply (如深) as possible regarding sensitive imperial matters. Tang Dynasty society expanded it to describe any collective agreement to maintain absolute silence about uncomfortable truths. The depth metaphor perfectly captured how some topics become so taboo that even acknowledging their existence becomes forbidden.
metaphoric_meaning: Maintain absolute silence about sensitive matter
description_tr: Han Dynasty court protocols first established this practice of avoiding discussion (諱莫) as deeply (如深) as possible regarding sensitive imperial matters. Tang Dynasty society expanded it to describe any collective agreement to maintain absolute silence about uncomfortable truths. The depth metaphor perfectly captured how some topics become so taboo that even acknowledging their existence becomes forbidden.
chineseExample_tr: 家人從不提及這一醜聞，將其視爲禁忌話題

example: The manager erupted in rage upon discovering the accounting discrepancy
chineseExample: 经理发现会计差异后，怒火中烧
description: Tang Dynasty theater directors first used this vivid image of anger as fire (火) rising (冒) three zhang (三丈) high - about 10 meters - in stage directions. Actors were instructed to physically manifest rage at this scale to convey its overwhelming nature. Ming Dynasty writers brought this theatrical metaphor into literature, where it perfectly captured those moments when provocation creates an explosive emotional response visible to all observers.
metaphoric_meaning: Explode in extreme anger or rage
description_tr: Tang Dynasty theater directors first used this vivid image of anger as fire (火) rising (冒) three zhang (三丈) high - about 10 meters - in stage directions. Actors were instructed to physically manifest rage at this scale to convey its overwhelming nature. Ming Dynasty writers brought this theatrical metaphor into literature, where it perfectly captured those moments when provocation creates an explosive emotional response visible to all observers.
chineseExample_tr: 經理發現會計差異後，怒火中燒

example: The hasty development project prioritized quick returns over quality and sustainability
chineseExample: 这个仓促的开发项目优先考虑快速回报，而非质量和可持续性
description: Tang Dynasty court critics first used this phrase to condemn officials rushing achievements (急功) for immediate benefits (近利). Song Dynasty Neo-Confucian philosophers expanded it into a broader warning about the dangers of prioritizing speed over proper development. The spatial metaphor of 'near' (近) benefits perfectly captured the shortsightedness of sacrificing lasting value for quick gains. Modern business ethics still wrestles with these same tensions.
metaphoric_meaning: Seek quick success and immediate benefit
description_tr: Tang Dynasty court critics first used this phrase to condemn officials rushing achievements (急功) for immediate benefits (近利). Song Dynasty Neo-Confucian philosophers expanded it into a broader warning about the dangers of prioritizing speed over proper development. The spatial metaphor of 'near' (近) benefits perfectly captured the shortsightedness of sacrificing lasting value for quick gains. Modern business ethics still wrestles with these same tensions.
chineseExample_tr: 這個倉促的開發項目優先考慮快速回報，而非質量和可持續性

example: She waited at the station, gazing through autumn waters for his return.
chineseExample: 她在车站望穿秋水，等待他的归来。
description: This idiom originates from Tang Dynasty poetry, where 'autumn waters' (秋水) was a literary metaphor for bright, clear eyes. The phrase describes looking so intently and longingly that one's gaze seems to pierce through clear autumn waters. It gained prominence through the works of poet Wang Bo and later appeared in the famous Yuan Dynasty drama 'The West Chamber' (西厢记). The idiom captures the intensity of longing and anticipation, particularly in romantic contexts. In modern usage, it describes eagerly awaiting someone's arrival or news, conveying deep emotional attachment and patient hope.
metaphoric_meaning: To eagerly await someone or something
description_tr: This idiom originates from Tang Dynasty poetry, where 'autumn waters' (秋水) was a literary metaphor for bright, clear eyes. The phrase describes looking so intently and longingly that one's gaze seems to pierce through clear autumn waters. It gained prominence through the works of poet Wang Bo and later appeared in the famous Yuan Dynasty drama 'The West Chamber' (西廂記). The idiom captures the intensity of longing and anticipation, particularly in romantic contexts. In modern usage, it describes eagerly awaiting someone's arrival or news, conveying deep emotional attachment and patient hope.
chineseExample_tr: 她在車站望穿秋水，等待他的歸來。

example: Losing that small investment was just one hair from nine oxen compared to his total wealth.
chineseExample: 损失那点小投资对他的总财富来说不过是九牛一毛。
description: This idiom comes from a letter written by the great Han Dynasty historian Sima Qian (司马迁) to his friend Ren An. After being castrated as punishment for defending a disgraced general, Sima Qian wrote that his death would be as insignificant as losing one hair from nine oxen - utterly negligible in the grand scheme of things. The phrase emphasizes extreme insignificance through vivid proportion. In modern usage, it describes something trivial compared to a larger whole, often used to minimize losses or contributions.
metaphoric_meaning: A drop in the bucket; negligible amount
description_tr: This idiom comes from a letter written by the great Han Dynasty historian Sima Qian (司馬遷) to his friend Ren An. After being castrated as punishment for defending a disgraced general, Sima Qian wrote that his death would be as insignificant as losing one hair from nine oxen - utterly negligible in the grand scheme of things. The phrase emphasizes extreme insignificance through vivid proportion. In modern usage, it describes something trivial compared to a larger whole, often used to minimize losses or contributions.
chineseExample_tr: 損失那點小投資對他的總財富來說不過是九牛一毛。

meaning: Connected in four directions, reaching in eight
example: Shanghai's subway system extends in all directions throughout the city.
chineseExample: 上海的地铁系统四通八达，覆盖整个城市。
description: This idiom originated during the Warring States period and appears in 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史记). It describes roads or passages that connect and extend in all directions - four (四) main directions and eight (八) secondary ones, covering all possible paths. Originally used to describe the strategic advantage of well-connected territories, it later expanded to describe any extensive network. Modern usage applies to transportation systems, communication networks, business connections, or any well-developed infrastructure that provides comprehensive access.
description_tr: This idiom originated during the Warring States period and appears in 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史記). It describes roads or passages that connect and extend in all directions - four (四) main directions and eight (八) secondary ones, covering all possible paths. Originally used to describe the strategic advantage of well-connected territories, it later expanded to describe any extensive network. Modern usage applies to transportation systems, communication networks, business connections, or any well-developed infrastructure that provides comprehensive access.
chineseExample_tr: 上海的地鐵系統四通八達，覆蓋整個城市。

meaning: Heaven is long, earth is lasting
example: Their friendship proved to be as enduring as heaven and earth.
chineseExample: 他们的友谊天长地久，永不改变。
description: This idiom derives from Chapter 7 of the Daoist classic 'Tao Te Ching' (道德经) by Laozi: 'Heaven and earth are long-lasting.' The phrase uses the eternal nature of heaven (天) and earth (地) as metaphors for permanence and durability. It gained romantic connotations through Tang Dynasty poetry, particularly Bai Juyi's 'Song of Everlasting Sorrow' where it describes enduring love. The idiom is commonly used in wedding blessings and expressions of lasting relationships, though it can apply to any bond or commitment meant to endure through time.
description_tr: This idiom derives from Chapter 7 of the Daoist classic 'Tao Te Ching' (道德經) by Laozi: 'Heaven and earth are long-lasting.' The phrase uses the eternal nature of heaven (天) and earth (地) as metaphors for permanence and durability. It gained romantic connotations through Tang Dynasty poetry, particularly Bai Juyi's 'Song of Everlasting Sorrow' where it describes enduring love. The idiom is commonly used in wedding blessings and expressions of lasting relationships, though it can apply to any bond or commitment meant to endure through time.
chineseExample_tr: 他們的友誼天長地久，永不改變。

example: The team wished her success from the moment she began.
chineseExample: 团队祝愿她马到成功。
description: This idiom originates from ancient Chinese military culture, where cavalry was crucial to victory. When war horses arrived at the battlefield, success was expected to follow swiftly. The phrase first appeared during the Yuan Dynasty in dramatic works describing military campaigns. The horse (马) symbolizes speed, power, and momentum, while 'arrival' (到) and 'success' (成功) emphasize immediate achievement. Today, it serves as a popular blessing for new ventures, examinations, and business endeavors, expressing hopes for swift and complete success.
description_tr: This idiom originates from ancient Chinese military culture, where cavalry was crucial to victory. When war horses arrived at the battlefield, success was expected to follow swiftly. The phrase first appeared during the Yuan Dynasty in dramatic works describing military campaigns. The horse (馬) symbolizes speed, power, and momentum, while 'arrival' (到) and 'success' (成功) emphasize immediate achievement. Today, it serves as a popular blessing for new ventures, examinations, and business endeavors, expressing hopes for swift and complete success.
chineseExample_tr: 團隊祝願她馬到成功。

meaning: Fighting for minutes and seizing seconds
example: The emergency team worked against time to save the patient.
chineseExample: 急救团队争分夺秒地抢救病人。
description: This modern idiom emerged during China's industrialization period in the 20th century, reflecting the urgency of national development. It combines 'fighting for' (争) minutes (分) and 'seizing' (夺) seconds (秒) to emphasize making the most of every moment. The phrase became particularly popular during the Great Leap Forward era and remains common in contexts requiring urgent action. It expresses the importance of efficiency and the race against time, commonly used in emergency situations, competitive environments, and deadline-driven work.
metaphoric_meaning: Race against time; make every second count
description_tr: This modern idiom emerged during China's industrialization period in the 20th century, reflecting the urgency of national development. It combines 'fighting for' (爭) minutes (分) and 'seizing' (奪) seconds (秒) to emphasize making the most of every moment. The phrase became particularly popular during the Great Leap Forward era and remains common in contexts requiring urgent action. It expresses the importance of efficiency and the race against time, commonly used in emergency situations, competitive environments, and deadline-driven work.
chineseExample_tr: 急救團隊爭分奪秒地搶救病人。

example: The market offers a bewildering variety of products.
chineseExample: 市场上的产品五花八门，令人眼花缭乱。
description: This idiom has roots in ancient Chinese military strategy, where 'five flowers' (五花) referred to five tactical formations and 'eight gates' (八门) to eight strategic positions. The phrase originally described the complexity of battlefield tactics. Over time, it evolved to describe any diverse or varied collection. Some scholars also trace it to the five-element theory and eight trigrams of Chinese philosophy. In modern usage, it describes a wide variety of things, often with a connotation of being overwhelming or confusingly diverse.
metaphoric_meaning: A bewildering variety; all kinds of
description_tr: This idiom has roots in ancient Chinese military strategy, where 'five flowers' (五花) referred to five tactical formations and 'eight gates' (八門) to eight strategic positions. The phrase originally described the complexity of battlefield tactics. Over time, it evolved to describe any diverse or varied collection. Some scholars also trace it to the five-element theory and eight trigrams of Chinese philosophy. In modern usage, it describes a wide variety of things, often with a connotation of being overwhelming or confusingly diverse.
chineseExample_tr: 市場上的產品五花八門，令人眼花繚亂。

example: A leader must always keep their promises to maintain credibility.
chineseExample: 一个领导者必须言而有信，才能保持信誉。
description: This idiom comes from 'The Analects of Confucius' (论语), where the sage emphasized that a person's words (言) must be accompanied by trustworthiness (信). In Confucian ethics, keeping one's word was considered a fundamental virtue essential for social harmony. The phrase encapsulates the idea that promises should be honored and speech should be reliable. It remains a core value in Chinese business and personal relationships, where verbal commitments carry significant moral weight.
metaphoric_meaning: To be true to one's word
description_tr: This idiom comes from 'The Analects of Confucius' (論語), where the sage emphasized that a person's words (言) must be accompanied by trustworthiness (信). In Confucian ethics, keeping one's word was considered a fundamental virtue essential for social harmony. The phrase encapsulates the idea that promises should be honored and speech should be reliable. It remains a core value in Chinese business and personal relationships, where verbal commitments carry significant moral weight.
chineseExample_tr: 一個領導者必須言而有信，才能保持信譽。

example: Learning a language requires a step-by-step approach.
chineseExample: 学习语言需要循序渐进的方法。
description: This idiom appears in 'The Analects of Confucius' and was elaborated upon by Zhu Xi, the great Song Dynasty Neo-Confucian scholar. It emphasizes following (循) a proper sequence (序) while advancing (进) gradually (渐). The phrase reflects the Confucian educational philosophy that learning must proceed systematically, building upon foundations before advancing to complex concepts. It counters the temptation to skip steps or rush progress. Modern usage extends to any field requiring methodical advancement, from education to career development to skill acquisition.
description_tr: This idiom appears in 'The Analects of Confucius' and was elaborated upon by Zhu Xi, the great Song Dynasty Neo-Confucian scholar. It emphasizes following (循) a proper sequence (序) while advancing (進) gradually (漸). The phrase reflects the Confucian educational philosophy that learning must proceed systematically, building upon foundations before advancing to complex concepts. It counters the temptation to skip steps or rush progress. Modern usage extends to any field requiring methodical advancement, from education to career development to skill acquisition.
chineseExample_tr: 學習語言需要循序漸進的方法。

example: The startup that entered the market last became the industry leader.
chineseExample: 最后进入市场的那家初创公司后来居上，成为行业领袖。
description: This idiom originates from 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史记), describing how later (后来) officials often rose (居上) to higher positions than their predecessors. The phrase was used by Emperor Wu of Han's advisor to explain why recently appointed ministers sometimes outranked senior ones. It suggests that timing of entry does not determine ultimate success. Modern usage celebrates underdogs and late starters who surpass early leaders through superior effort or strategy, particularly relevant in competitive markets and sports.
description_tr: This idiom originates from 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史記), describing how later (後來) officials often rose (居上) to higher positions than their predecessors. The phrase was used by Emperor Wu of Han's advisor to explain why recently appointed ministers sometimes outranked senior ones. It suggests that timing of entry does not determine ultimate success. Modern usage celebrates underdogs and late starters who surpass early leaders through superior effort or strategy, particularly relevant in competitive markets and sports.
chineseExample_tr: 最後進入市場的那家初創公司後來居上，成為行業領袖。

example: The restaurant truly lives up to its reputation as the best in town.
chineseExample: 这家餐厅名副其实，确实是城里最好的。
description: This idiom originates from the Han Dynasty text 'Hou Han Shu' (后汉书), where it described officials whose abilities matched their titles. The phrase combines 'name' (名) with 'matching' (副) its 'reality' (实). In ancient China, there was great emphasis on the correspondence between titles and actual merit, as misaligned names and realities were seen as a source of social disorder. Confucius himself emphasized 'rectification of names' (正名). Modern usage applies to anything that lives up to its reputation, from products to people to places.
metaphoric_meaning: Living up to one's name or reputation
description_tr: This idiom originates from the Han Dynasty text 'Hou Han Shu' (後漢書), where it described officials whose abilities matched their titles. The phrase combines 'name' (名) with 'matching' (副) its 'reality' (實). In ancient China, there was great emphasis on the correspondence between titles and actual merit, as misaligned names and realities were seen as a source of social disorder. Confucius himself emphasized 'rectification of names' (正名). Modern usage applies to anything that lives up to its reputation, from products to people to places.
chineseExample_tr: 這家餐廳名副其實，確實是城裡最好的。

meaning: Heart's blood comes in a tide
example: On a whim, she decided to book a flight to Paris.
chineseExample: 她心血来潮，决定订一张去巴黎的机票。
description: This idiom comes from traditional Chinese medicine concepts where the heart (心) was believed to govern blood (血) circulation and emotions. When blood rushes to the heart like a tide (潮), it was thought to cause sudden impulses or inspirations. The phrase gained literary currency during the Ming Dynasty in novels describing characters acting on sudden urges. In modern usage, it describes impulsive decisions or sudden whims, carrying a neutral to slightly cautionary tone about acting without deliberation.
metaphoric_meaning: Acting on a sudden impulse or whim
description_tr: This idiom comes from traditional Chinese medicine concepts where the heart (心) was believed to govern blood (血) circulation and emotions. When blood rushes to the heart like a tide (潮), it was thought to cause sudden impulses or inspirations. The phrase gained literary currency during the Ming Dynasty in novels describing characters acting on sudden urges. In modern usage, it describes impulsive decisions or sudden whims, carrying a neutral to slightly cautionary tone about acting without deliberation.
chineseExample_tr: 她心血來潮，決定訂一張去巴黎的機票。

example: The technological revolution is an unstoppable force reshaping society.
chineseExample: 科技革命势不可挡，正在重塑社会。
description: This idiom originated in military contexts during the Three Kingdoms period, describing armies with such overwhelming momentum (势) that they could not (不可) be blocked or resisted (挡). The concept of 'shi' (势) is central to Chinese strategic thinking, representing the dynamic force or momentum of a situation. Sunzi's 'Art of War' extensively discusses harnessing and creating unstoppable momentum. Modern usage extends to any powerful trend, movement, or force that cannot be resisted, from market forces to social changes.
description_tr: This idiom originated in military contexts during the Three Kingdoms period, describing armies with such overwhelming momentum (勢) that they could not (不可) be blocked or resisted (擋). The concept of 'shi' (勢) is central to Chinese strategic thinking, representing the dynamic force or momentum of a situation. Sunzi's 'Art of War' extensively discusses harnessing and creating unstoppable momentum. Modern usage extends to any powerful trend, movement, or force that cannot be resisted, from market forces to social changes.
chineseExample_tr: 科技革命勢不可擋，正在重塑社會。

example: The redesigned website gave visitors a refreshing new experience.
chineseExample: 重新设计的网站让访客耳目一新。
description: This idiom appears in classical texts describing the sensation of encountering something refreshingly new. The ears (耳) and eyes (目) represent one's senses and perception, while 'completely renewed' (一新) suggests a total refresh of one's experience. The phrase emphasizes the delight of fresh perspectives after encountering innovative ideas, places, or creations. It celebrates novelty and the pleasure of having one's expectations pleasantly subverted. Modern usage commonly describes innovative designs, fresh approaches, or transformative experiences.
description_tr: This idiom appears in classical texts describing the sensation of encountering something refreshingly new. The ears (耳) and eyes (目) represent one's senses and perception, while 'completely renewed' (一新) suggests a total refresh of one's experience. The phrase emphasizes the delight of fresh perspectives after encountering innovative ideas, places, or creations. It celebrates novelty and the pleasure of having one's expectations pleasantly subverted. Modern usage commonly describes innovative designs, fresh approaches, or transformative experiences.
chineseExample_tr: 重新設計的網站讓訪客耳目一新。

example: His business strategy was completely counterproductive to his stated goals.
chineseExample: 他的商业策略与既定目标南辕北辙。
description: This idiom comes from 'Strategies of the Warring States' (战国策). A man wanted to travel to the southern state of Chu but drove his carriage north. When asked why, he boasted of his fast horses, skilled driver, and ample provisions, failing to realize that these advantages would only take him further from his destination. The story illustrates how having resources means nothing if one's direction is fundamentally wrong. Modern usage describes actions that are counterproductive to stated intentions or methods that contradict desired outcomes.
description_tr: This idiom comes from 'Strategies of the Warring States' (戰國策). A man wanted to travel to the southern state of Chu but drove his carriage north. When asked why, he boasted of his fast horses, skilled driver, and ample provisions, failing to realize that these advantages would only take him further from his destination. The story illustrates how having resources means nothing if one's direction is fundamentally wrong. Modern usage describes actions that are counterproductive to stated intentions or methods that contradict desired outcomes.
chineseExample_tr: 他的商業策略與既定目標南轅北轍。

example: Success comes from practical, grounded efforts rather than empty dreams.
chineseExample: 成功来自脚踏实地的努力，而非空想。
description: This idiom originated during the Song Dynasty in biographical accounts praising scholars who pursued knowledge through steady, practical effort rather than seeking shortcuts. The image of feet (脚) firmly stepping (踏) on solid (实) ground (地) contrasts with those who have their heads in the clouds. The phrase embodies the Confucian value of earnest effort and realistic approach to achievement. It remains particularly popular in educational and professional contexts, encouraging steady work over flashy but insubstantial approaches.
description_tr: This idiom originated during the Song Dynasty in biographical accounts praising scholars who pursued knowledge through steady, practical effort rather than seeking shortcuts. The image of feet (腳) firmly stepping (踏) on solid (實) ground (地) contrasts with those who have their heads in the clouds. The phrase embodies the Confucian value of earnest effort and realistic approach to achievement. It remains particularly popular in educational and professional contexts, encouraging steady work over flashy but insubstantial approaches.
chineseExample_tr: 成功來自腳踏實地的努力，而非空想。

example: She dedicated herself wholeheartedly to her research.
chineseExample: 她一心一意地投入研究工作。
description: This idiom appears in Buddhist texts translated during the Han Dynasty, describing the focused mind required for meditation. The repetition of 'one' (一) with 'heart' (心) and 'mind/intention' (意) emphasizes complete unity of purpose and concentration. The phrase gained broader usage in describing devotion in any endeavor. It represents the ideal state of undivided attention and commitment, whether in spiritual practice, work, or relationships. Modern usage emphasizes wholehearted dedication and single-minded focus on a goal or person.
description_tr: This idiom appears in Buddhist texts translated during the Han Dynasty, describing the focused mind required for meditation. The repetition of 'one' (一) with 'heart' (心) and 'mind/intention' (意) emphasizes complete unity of purpose and concentration. The phrase gained broader usage in describing devotion in any endeavor. It represents the ideal state of undivided attention and commitment, whether in spiritual practice, work, or relationships. Modern usage emphasizes wholehearted dedication and single-minded focus on a goal or person.
chineseExample_tr: 她一心一意地投入研究工作。

example: Most smartphones today are largely similar with only minor differences.
chineseExample: 现在的智能手机大同小异，只有细微差别。
description: This idiom has roots in the Confucian concept of 'datong' (大同), meaning 'great unity' or 'grand harmony,' which described an ideal society. The phrase acknowledges that while things may appear different on the surface, they share fundamental similarities. The contrast between 'large' (大) sameness (同) and 'small' (小) differences (异) suggests that what unites is more significant than what divides. Modern usage describes things that are essentially alike despite superficial variations, often used when comparing products, ideas, or approaches.
metaphoric_meaning: Essentially the same with minor differences
description_tr: This idiom has roots in the Confucian concept of 'datong' (大同), meaning 'great unity' or 'grand harmony,' which described an ideal society. The phrase acknowledges that while things may appear different on the surface, they share fundamental similarities. The contrast between 'large' (大) sameness (同) and 'small' (小) differences (異) suggests that what unites is more significant than what divides. Modern usage describes things that are essentially alike despite superficial variations, often used when comparing products, ideas, or approaches.
chineseExample_tr: 現在的智能手機大同小異，只有細微差別。

example: Behind the modest entrance lies a surprisingly beautiful garden.
chineseExample: 朴素的入口后面别有洞天，是一个美丽的花园。
description: This idiom derives from Daoist mythology, where 'cave heavens' (洞天) were believed to be paradisiacal realms hidden within mountains, accessible only to immortals or the spiritually enlightened. These hidden worlds represented a different (别) reality existing alongside the mundane world. The phrase first appeared in Tang Dynasty poetry describing scenic discoveries. Modern usage describes finding unexpected beauty, depth, or interest in something that appears ordinary from the outside, celebrating hidden wonders and pleasant surprises.
metaphoric_meaning: A hidden paradise; unexpected inner beauty
description_tr: This idiom derives from Daoist mythology, where 'cave heavens' (洞天) were believed to be paradisiacal realms hidden within mountains, accessible only to immortals or the spiritually enlightened. These hidden worlds represented a different (別) reality existing alongside the mundane world. The phrase first appeared in Tang Dynasty poetry describing scenic discoveries. Modern usage describes finding unexpected beauty, depth, or interest in something that appears ordinary from the outside, celebrating hidden wonders and pleasant surprises.
chineseExample_tr: 樸素的入口後面別有洞天，是一個美麗的花園。

meaning: Without any way to deal with it
example: Faced with the natural disaster, the villagers felt helpless.
chineseExample: 面对自然灾害，村民们无可奈何。
description: This idiom appears in early Chinese philosophical texts and gained literary fame through the Song Dynasty poet Yan Shu's famous line about falling flowers. The phrase expresses the feeling of being without (无) any possible (可) way to cope (奈何) with a situation. It captures the universal human experience of helplessness before circumstances beyond one's control, whether natural forces, fate, or the passage of time. Modern usage expresses resignation and acceptance when facing insurmountable obstacles or inevitable outcomes.
description_tr: This idiom appears in early Chinese philosophical texts and gained literary fame through the Song Dynasty poet Yan Shu's famous line about falling flowers. The phrase expresses the feeling of being without (無) any possible (可) way to cope (奈何) with a situation. It captures the universal human experience of helplessness before circumstances beyond one's control, whether natural forces, fate, or the passage of time. Modern usage expresses resignation and acceptance when facing insurmountable obstacles or inevitable outcomes.
chineseExample_tr: 面對自然災害，村民們無可奈何。

meaning: Chicken flies away and eggs broken
example: His reckless investment left him with nothing - the chicken flew and the eggs broke.
chineseExample: 他冒险的投资让他鸡飞蛋打，一无所有。
description: This vivid idiom depicts the scene of trying to catch a chicken and accidentally breaking its eggs in the process - losing both the bird and its potential offspring. It emerged from rural Chinese life where chickens represented valuable assets. The phrase captures the frustration of a double loss, particularly when greed or poor judgment causes one to lose everything. Modern usage describes situations where attempting to gain something results in losing what one already had, emphasizing the danger of overreaching.
description_tr: This vivid idiom depicts the scene of trying to catch a chicken and accidentally breaking its eggs in the process - losing both the bird and its potential offspring. It emerged from rural Chinese life where chickens represented valuable assets. The phrase captures the frustration of a double loss, particularly when greed or poor judgment causes one to lose everything. Modern usage describes situations where attempting to gain something results in losing what one already had, emphasizing the danger of overreaching.
chineseExample_tr: 他冒險的投資讓他雞飛蛋打，一無所有。

example: His carelessness caused the crucial error in the report.
chineseExample: 他的粗心大意导致报告中出现了关键错误。
description: This idiom combines 'rough/coarse' (粗) heart (心) with 'big/careless' (大) intention (意) to describe a lack of attention to detail. While both components independently suggest carelessness, together they emphasize habitual inattention rather than momentary lapses. The phrase gained currency in Ming Dynasty moral instruction texts warning against neglecting details. It remains commonly used in educational and professional settings to describe someone who makes avoidable mistakes due to insufficient care and attention.
description_tr: This idiom combines 'rough/coarse' (粗) heart (心) with 'big/careless' (大) intention (意) to describe a lack of attention to detail. While both components independently suggest carelessness, together they emphasize habitual inattention rather than momentary lapses. The phrase gained currency in Ming Dynasty moral instruction texts warning against neglecting details. It remains commonly used in educational and professional settings to describe someone who makes avoidable mistakes due to insufficient care and attention.
chineseExample_tr: 他的粗心大意導致報告中出現了關鍵錯誤。

example: His arrogance prevents him from accepting any criticism.
chineseExample: 他自以为是，无法接受任何批评。
description: This idiom appears in the ancient Daoist text 'Zhuangzi' and was used by Confucius to criticize those who considered themselves (自以为) always right (是). The phrase critiques the dangerous combination of confidence and closed-mindedness. In Chinese philosophical tradition, true wisdom requires humility and openness to correction. The idiom warns against the intellectual arrogance that prevents growth and learning. Modern usage describes someone who is opinionated, self-righteous, or unwilling to consider alternative viewpoints.
description_tr: This idiom appears in the ancient Daoist text 'Zhuangzi' and was used by Confucius to criticize those who considered themselves (自以為) always right (是). The phrase critiques the dangerous combination of confidence and closed-mindedness. In Chinese philosophical tradition, true wisdom requires humility and openness to correction. The idiom warns against the intellectual arrogance that prevents growth and learning. Modern usage describes someone who is opinionated, self-righteous, or unwilling to consider alternative viewpoints.
chineseExample_tr: 他自以為是，無法接受任何批評。

example: The championship game was his chance to showcase his skills.
chineseExample: 冠军赛是他大显身手的机会。
description: This idiom combines 'greatly display' (大显) with 'body and hands' (身手), where 'shenshou' represents one's physical abilities and skills. The phrase originated in martial arts contexts during the Ming Dynasty, describing warriors demonstrating their combat prowess. The theatrical element of 'display' suggests not just using abilities but showcasing them impressively. Modern usage extends to any context where someone has an opportunity to demonstrate their talents and skills, whether in sports, business, or creative endeavors.
description_tr: This idiom combines 'greatly display' (大顯) with 'body and hands' (身手), where 'shenshou' represents one's physical abilities and skills. The phrase originated in martial arts contexts during the Ming Dynasty, describing warriors demonstrating their combat prowess. The theatrical element of 'display' suggests not just using abilities but showcasing them impressively. Modern usage extends to any context where someone has an opportunity to demonstrate their talents and skills, whether in sports, business, or creative endeavors.
chineseExample_tr: 冠軍賽是他大顯身手的機會。

example: The army was utterly defeated and scattered in all directions.
chineseExample: 军队被打得落花流水，四处逃散。
description: This poetic idiom originally described the beautiful natural scene of fallen petals floating on flowing streams, appearing in Tang Dynasty poetry as an image of transient beauty. However, its meaning evolved to describe utter defeat or disarray, like flowers scattered helplessly by water currents. The transformation from aesthetic appreciation to military metaphor occurred during the Song Dynasty. Modern usage primarily describes crushing defeats or chaotic retreats, though it occasionally retains its original poetic sense in literary contexts.
description_tr: This poetic idiom originally described the beautiful natural scene of fallen petals floating on flowing streams, appearing in Tang Dynasty poetry as an image of transient beauty. However, its meaning evolved to describe utter defeat or disarray, like flowers scattered helplessly by water currents. The transformation from aesthetic appreciation to military metaphor occurred during the Song Dynasty. Modern usage primarily describes crushing defeats or chaotic retreats, though it occasionally retains its original poetic sense in literary contexts.
chineseExample_tr: 軍隊被打得落花流水，四處逃散。

example: The children listened to the story with great enthusiasm.
chineseExample: 孩子们津津有味地听着故事。
description: This idiom uses the reduplicated 'jinjin' (津津), meaning moist or flowing with saliva, combined with 'having flavor' (有味) to describe intense enjoyment. The salivation metaphor connects to the physical response of anticipating delicious food. First appearing in Ming Dynasty literature, the phrase described both literal enjoyment of food and metaphorical relish for any engaging experience. Modern usage emphasizes enthusiastic engagement, whether reading a book, listening to a story, or pursuing any absorbing activity.
description_tr: This idiom uses the reduplicated 'jinjin' (津津), meaning moist or flowing with saliva, combined with 'having flavor' (有味) to describe intense enjoyment. The salivation metaphor connects to the physical response of anticipating delicious food. First appearing in Ming Dynasty literature, the phrase described both literal enjoyment of food and metaphorical relish for any engaging experience. Modern usage emphasizes enthusiastic engagement, whether reading a book, listening to a story, or pursuing any absorbing activity.
chineseExample_tr: 孩子們津津有味地聽著故事。

example: Don't limit yourself by drawing boundaries that don't exist.
chineseExample: 不要画地为牢，限制自己的发展。
description: This idiom originates from 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史记), describing ancient punishment where criminals were confined to a circle drawn on the ground, honoring the boundary as if it were prison walls. The story illustrates how social trust and honor could substitute for physical restraints. The phrase has evolved to describe self-imposed limitations, where people restrict themselves within imaginary boundaries. Modern usage warns against creating unnecessary mental barriers that limit one's potential and opportunities.
description_tr: This idiom originates from 'Records of the Grand Historian' (史記), describing ancient punishment where criminals were confined to a circle drawn on the ground, honoring the boundary as if it were prison walls. The story illustrates how social trust and honor could substitute for physical restraints. The phrase has evolved to describe self-imposed limitations, where people restrict themselves within imaginary boundaries. Modern usage warns against creating unnecessary mental barriers that limit one's potential and opportunities.
chineseExample_tr: 不要畫地為牢，限制自己的發展。

example: Let's discuss this matter calmly and rationally.
chineseExample: 让我们心平气和地讨论这件事。
description: This idiom combines 'calm heart' (心平) with 'harmonious breath/energy' (气和), reflecting traditional Chinese understanding of emotional and physical balance. In Chinese medicine and philosophy, the state of one's 'qi' (气) directly affects emotional disposition. The phrase appears in Song Dynasty Neo-Confucian texts promoting emotional equilibrium as essential for moral cultivation. It represents the ideal state for rational discourse and decision-making. Modern usage encourages composed, level-headed communication, especially in potentially contentious situations.
description_tr: This idiom combines 'calm heart' (心平) with 'harmonious breath/energy' (氣和), reflecting traditional Chinese understanding of emotional and physical balance. In Chinese medicine and philosophy, the state of one's 'qi' (氣) directly affects emotional disposition. The phrase appears in Song Dynasty Neo-Confucian texts promoting emotional equilibrium as essential for moral cultivation. It represents the ideal state for rational discourse and decision-making. Modern usage encourages composed, level-headed communication, especially in potentially contentious situations.
chineseExample_tr: 讓我們心平氣和地討論這件事。

example: Everyone was talking at once, offering different opinions.
chineseExample: 大家七嘴八舌，各抒己见。
description: This idiom uses the numbers seven (七) and eight (八), which in Chinese often suggest 'many' or 'various,' combined with mouths (嘴) and tongues (舌) to describe multiple people talking simultaneously. The phrase emerged during the Ming Dynasty in colloquial literature depicting lively scenes of debate or gossip. It captures the chaos and energy of multiple voices competing to be heard. Modern usage describes animated group discussions, often with a slightly chaotic or noisy connotation.
metaphoric_meaning: Everyone talking at once; lively discussion
description_tr: This idiom uses the numbers seven (七) and eight (八), which in Chinese often suggest 'many' or 'various,' combined with mouths (嘴) and tongues (舌) to describe multiple people talking simultaneously. The phrase emerged during the Ming Dynasty in colloquial literature depicting lively scenes of debate or gossip. It captures the chaos and energy of multiple voices competing to be heard. Modern usage describes animated group discussions, often with a slightly chaotic or noisy connotation.
chineseExample_tr: 大家七嘴八舌，各抒己見。

example: Her response was perfectly appropriate for the situation.
chineseExample: 她的回应恰如其分，非常得体。
description: This idiom emphasizes doing something exactly (恰) as (如) its proper portion (其分) requires - neither more nor less. The concept of 'fen' (分) relates to the Confucian idea that everything has its proper measure and place. The phrase praises the virtue of appropriateness and moderation, avoiding both excess and deficiency. It reflects the classical Chinese aesthetic of balance and propriety. Modern usage commends actions, words, or responses that are perfectly suited to their context and neither overdone nor insufficient.
description_tr: This idiom emphasizes doing something exactly (恰) as (如) its proper portion (其分) requires - neither more nor less. The concept of 'fen' (分) relates to the Confucian idea that everything has its proper measure and place. The phrase praises the virtue of appropriateness and moderation, avoiding both excess and deficiency. It reflects the classical Chinese aesthetic of balance and propriety. Modern usage commends actions, words, or responses that are perfectly suited to their context and neither overdone nor insufficient.
chineseExample_tr: 她的回應恰如其分，非常得體。

example: He is known for his upright character and transparent dealings.
chineseExample: 他以光明磊落的品格和透明的处事方式著称。
description: This idiom combines 'bright and luminous' (光明) with 'open and upright' (磊落) to describe someone of impeccable moral character. The character 磊 depicts stones piled openly, suggesting nothing hidden. The phrase appears in Song Dynasty biographical texts praising officials who conducted themselves with complete transparency and integrity. It embodies the Confucian ideal of the junzi (gentleman) who has nothing to hide and whose actions can withstand scrutiny. Modern usage commends honest, forthright individuals in personal and professional contexts.
description_tr: This idiom combines 'bright and luminous' (光明) with 'open and upright' (磊落) to describe someone of impeccable moral character. The character 磊 depicts stones piled openly, suggesting nothing hidden. The phrase appears in Song Dynasty biographical texts praising officials who conducted themselves with complete transparency and integrity. It embodies the Confucian ideal of the junzi (gentleman) who has nothing to hide and whose actions can withstand scrutiny. Modern usage commends honest, forthright individuals in personal and professional contexts.
chineseExample_tr: 他以光明磊落的品格和透明的處事方式著稱。

example: Her writing was so vivid that the scenes seemed to bloom from the page.
chineseExample: 她的文笔妙笔生花，场景仿佛从纸上绽放。
description: This idiom originates from the legend of the Tang Dynasty poet Li Bai, who allegedly dreamed that flowers bloomed from his writing brush, after which his poetry became extraordinarily beautiful. The 'wonderful brush' (妙笔) producing flowers (生花) symbolizes exceptional literary talent that brings words to life. The phrase celebrates the magical quality of excellent writing that transforms ordinary words into vivid imagery. Modern usage praises exceptional writing ability, eloquent expression, or creative skill in any artistic medium.
description_tr: This idiom originates from the legend of the Tang Dynasty poet Li Bai, who allegedly dreamed that flowers bloomed from his writing brush, after which his poetry became extraordinarily beautiful. The 'wonderful brush' (妙筆) producing flowers (生花) symbolizes exceptional literary talent that brings words to life. The phrase celebrates the magical quality of excellent writing that transforms ordinary words into vivid imagery. Modern usage praises exceptional writing ability, eloquent expression, or creative skill in any artistic medium.
chineseExample_tr: 她的文筆妙筆生花，場景仿佛從紙上綻放。

example: Tech startups are emerging rapidly like bamboo shoots after rain.
chineseExample: 科技初创公司如雨后春笋般涌现。
description: This idiom captures the natural phenomenon of bamboo shoots (春笋) growing rapidly after (后) spring rain (雨). In Chinese landscapes, bamboo groves visibly transform after rainfall as countless new shoots emerge almost overnight. The phrase first appeared in Song Dynasty texts describing rapid multiplication of phenomena. It emphasizes both speed and abundance, suggesting organic, natural growth rather than forced development. Modern usage commonly describes rapid emergence of new businesses, trends, buildings, or any phenomenon proliferating quickly.
description_tr: This idiom captures the natural phenomenon of bamboo shoots (春筍) growing rapidly after (後) spring rain (雨). In Chinese landscapes, bamboo groves visibly transform after rainfall as countless new shoots emerge almost overnight. The phrase first appeared in Song Dynasty texts describing rapid multiplication of phenomena. It emphasizes both speed and abundance, suggesting organic, natural growth rather than forced development. Modern usage commonly describes rapid emergence of new businesses, trends, buildings, or any phenomenon proliferating quickly.
chineseExample_tr: 科技初創公司如雨後春筍般湧現。

example: She was overjoyed when she received the unexpected promotion.
chineseExample: 收到意外的晋升通知，她喜出望外。
description: This idiom describes joy (喜) that goes beyond (出) one's expectations (望外). It captures the special quality of pleasant surprises that exceed what one hoped for or anticipated. The phrase appeared in Song Dynasty literature describing characters receiving unexpectedly good news. The emotional intensity it conveys goes beyond simple happiness to suggest delighted astonishment. Modern usage describes any situation where positive outcomes surpass expectations, from personal achievements to fortunate discoveries.
description_tr: This idiom describes joy (喜) that goes beyond (出) one's expectations (望外). It captures the special quality of pleasant surprises that exceed what one hoped for or anticipated. The phrase appeared in Song Dynasty literature describing characters receiving unexpectedly good news. The emotional intensity it conveys goes beyond simple happiness to suggest delighted astonishment. Modern usage describes any situation where positive outcomes surpass expectations, from personal achievements to fortunate discoveries.
chineseExample_tr: 收到意外的晉升通知，她喜出望外。

example: He ignored the risks and pursued his dream regardless.
chineseExample: 他将风险置之度外，执着追求自己的梦想。
description: This idiom means to place (置) something (之) outside (外) the bounds of consideration (度). It originates from historical texts describing generals and heroes who disregarded personal safety for greater causes. The phrase emphasizes deliberate exclusion of certain factors - often personal risk, comfort, or gain - from one's decision-making. While sometimes used critically for recklessness, it more often praises courage and commitment that transcends self-interest. Modern usage describes ignoring obstacles or dangers in pursuit of important goals.
metaphoric_meaning: To disregard; put aside consideration of
description_tr: This idiom means to place (置) something (之) outside (外) the bounds of consideration (度). It originates from historical texts describing generals and heroes who disregarded personal safety for greater causes. The phrase emphasizes deliberate exclusion of certain factors - often personal risk, comfort, or gain - from one's decision-making. While sometimes used critically for recklessness, it more often praises courage and commitment that transcends self-interest. Modern usage describes ignoring obstacles or dangers in pursuit of important goals.
chineseExample_tr: 他將風險置之度外，執著追求自己的夢想。

example: After years of training, she can now manage the department independently.
chineseExample: 经过多年培训，她现在能够独当一面，管理整个部门。
description: This idiom originates from military contexts in the Han Dynasty, describing generals capable of independently (独) managing (当) one front or flank (一面) of battle. The phrase recognized the rare ability to take full responsibility for a significant area without supervision. It evolved to praise anyone capable of handling substantial responsibility independently. Modern usage commonly describes professionals who have matured to the point of managing projects, departments, or situations without requiring guidance.
description_tr: This idiom originates from military contexts in the Han Dynasty, describing generals capable of independently (獨) managing (當) one front or flank (一面) of battle. The phrase recognized the rare ability to take full responsibility for a significant area without supervision. It evolved to praise anyone capable of handling substantial responsibility independently. Modern usage commonly describes professionals who have matured to the point of managing projects, departments, or situations without requiring guidance.
chineseExample_tr: 經過多年培訓，她現在能夠獨當一面，管理整個部門。

example: Waiting for the exam results made her extremely restless.
chineseExample: 等待考试结果让她坐立不安。
description: This idiom describes the physical manifestation of anxiety where one can neither sit (坐) nor stand (立) comfortably, finding no peace (不安) in any position. The phrase captures the restless energy of worry that prevents physical stillness. It appears in classical Chinese literature describing characters in suspense or distress. The emphasis on bodily experience reflects traditional Chinese understanding of the mind-body connection. Modern usage describes anxiety, nervousness, or impatience that manifests in physical restlessness.
description_tr: This idiom describes the physical manifestation of anxiety where one can neither sit (坐) nor stand (立) comfortably, finding no peace (不安) in any position. The phrase captures the restless energy of worry that prevents physical stillness. It appears in classical Chinese literature describing characters in suspense or distress. The emphasis on bodily experience reflects traditional Chinese understanding of the mind-body connection. Modern usage describes anxiety, nervousness, or impatience that manifests in physical restlessness.
chineseExample_tr: 等待考試結果讓她坐立不安。

example: Chinese civilization has ancient roots and continues to thrive today.
chineseExample: 中华文明源远流长，延续至今。
description: This idiom uses the metaphor of a river with distant (远) sources (源) and long (长) flow (流) to describe things with deep historical roots and lasting influence. The phrase appeared in Confucian commentaries praising traditions that connect past to present through unbroken transmission. It emphasizes both antiquity and continuity - not just old, but enduringly vital. Modern usage commonly describes cultural traditions, academic disciplines, family lineages, or any institution with deep historical foundations that remain relevant.
metaphoric_meaning: Having deep roots and long history
description_tr: This idiom uses the metaphor of a river with distant (遠) sources (源) and long (長) flow (流) to describe things with deep historical roots and lasting influence. The phrase appeared in Confucian commentaries praising traditions that connect past to present through unbroken transmission. It emphasizes both antiquity and continuity - not just old, but enduringly vital. Modern usage commonly describes cultural traditions, academic disciplines, family lineages, or any institution with deep historical foundations that remain relevant.
chineseExample_tr: 中華文明源遠流長，延續至今。

example: The two friends are inseparable, always seen together.
chineseExample: 这两个朋友形影不离，总是在一起。
description: This idiom compares close companions to a person (形, form/body) and their shadow (影) that never (不) separate (离). The phrase originates from ancient Chinese poetry expressing the pain of loneliness, where one had only their shadow for company. It evolved to describe the most intimate of relationships where two people are constantly together. The physical impossibility of separating from one's shadow emphasizes the strength of the bond. Modern usage describes best friends, devoted couples, or any inseparable pair.
description_tr: This idiom compares close companions to a person (形, form/body) and their shadow (影) that never (不) separate (離). The phrase originates from ancient Chinese poetry expressing the pain of loneliness, where one had only their shadow for company. It evolved to describe the most intimate of relationships where two people are constantly together. The physical impossibility of separating from one's shadow emphasizes the strength of the bond. Modern usage describes best friends, devoted couples, or any inseparable pair.
chineseExample_tr: 這兩個朋友形影不離，總是在一起。

example: She was skeptical about his explanation but couldn't dismiss it entirely.
chineseExample: 她对他的解释半信半疑。
description: This idiom precisely captures the state of partial belief (半信) mixed with partial doubt (半疑), describing uncertainty where one can neither fully accept nor completely reject something. The balanced structure of 'half-half' reflects the equilibrium of competing inclinations. The phrase appears in Ming Dynasty vernacular literature depicting characters receiving incredible news. It represents a mature, cautious response to uncertain information. Modern usage describes healthy skepticism or the natural state of processing unverified claims.
description_tr: This idiom precisely captures the state of partial belief (半信) mixed with partial doubt (半疑), describing uncertainty where one can neither fully accept nor completely reject something. The balanced structure of 'half-half' reflects the equilibrium of competing inclinations. The phrase appears in Ming Dynasty vernacular literature depicting characters receiving incredible news. It represents a mature, cautious response to uncertain information. Modern usage describes healthy skepticism or the natural state of processing unverified claims.
chineseExample_tr: 她對他的解釋半信半疑。

example: It was a perfect day for the outdoor wedding ceremony.
chineseExample: 风和日丽的天气非常适合户外婚礼。
description: This idiom describes ideal weather conditions with gentle (和) wind (风) and beautiful (丽) sunshine (日). It first appeared in Tang Dynasty poetry celebrating perfect spring days. The phrase captures the Chinese aesthetic appreciation for harmonious natural conditions, where elements are balanced rather than extreme. Beyond literal weather description, it often sets a positive tone for events or serves as a metaphor for favorable circumstances. Modern usage commonly appears in event descriptions, travel writing, and as a general expression of pleasant conditions.
description_tr: This idiom describes ideal weather conditions with gentle (和) wind (風) and beautiful (麗) sunshine (日). It first appeared in Tang Dynasty poetry celebrating perfect spring days. The phrase captures the Chinese aesthetic appreciation for harmonious natural conditions, where elements are balanced rather than extreme. Beyond literal weather description, it often sets a positive tone for events or serves as a metaphor for favorable circumstances. Modern usage commonly appears in event descriptions, travel writing, and as a general expression of pleasant conditions.
chineseExample_tr: 風和日麗的天氣非常適合戶外婚禮。

meaning: Worry about gain, worry about loss
example: His constant anxiety about success prevented him from enjoying the present.
chineseExample: 他患得患失的心态让他无法享受当下。
description: This idiom comes from 'The Analects of Confucius,' where it describes petty people who worry (患) about gaining (得) before they have something, then worry about losing (失) it once obtained. Confucius contrasted this anxious mindset with the equanimity of the superior person. The phrase critiques attachment to outcomes that prevents peace of mind. It reflects Buddhist and Daoist influences on Chinese philosophy regarding non-attachment. Modern usage describes anxious, calculating mindsets that undermine happiness and effectiveness.
metaphoric_meaning: Constantly anxious about gains and losses
description_tr: This idiom comes from 'The Analects of Confucius,' where it describes petty people who worry (患) about gaining (得) before they have something, then worry about losing (失) it once obtained. Confucius contrasted this anxious mindset with the equanimity of the superior person. The phrase critiques attachment to outcomes that prevents peace of mind. It reflects Buddhist and Daoist influences on Chinese philosophy regarding non-attachment. Modern usage describes anxious, calculating mindsets that undermine happiness and effectiveness.
chineseExample_tr: 他患得患失的心態讓他無法享受當下。

example: Her performance was incomparable, far surpassing all others.
chineseExample: 她的表演无与伦比，远超其他人。
description: This idiom states that nothing (无) exists to (与) compare (伦比) with something, indicating supreme excellence. The character 伦 relates to categories or ranks, suggesting that the subject transcends normal classification. The phrase appeared in Han Dynasty texts praising exceptional individuals or achievements that stood beyond comparison. It represents the highest form of praise in Chinese rhetoric. Modern usage describes outstanding quality, performance, or achievement that cannot be matched by peers or competitors.
description_tr: This idiom states that nothing (無) exists to (與) compare (倫比) with something, indicating supreme excellence. The character 倫 relates to categories or ranks, suggesting that the subject transcends normal classification. The phrase appeared in Han Dynasty texts praising exceptional individuals or achievements that stood beyond comparison. It represents the highest form of praise in Chinese rhetoric. Modern usage describes outstanding quality, performance, or achievement that cannot be matched by peers or competitors.
chineseExample_tr: 她的表演無與倫比，遠超其他人。

meaning: Split apart upon meeting the blade
example: Once we identified the root cause, the problem was easily solved.
chineseExample: 一旦找到根本原因，问题就迎刃而解了。
description: This idiom originates from the Jin Dynasty general Du Yu's description of his military campaign, where he likened enemy resistance to bamboo that splits upon meeting (迎) the blade (刃). Once the first joint splits, the rest follows easily (而解). The phrase emphasizes how problems, once properly approached, often resolve with surprising ease. It suggests finding the right approach or leverage point that makes seemingly difficult challenges straightforward. Modern usage describes problems that dissolve once the correct solution is applied.
description_tr: This idiom originates from the Jin Dynasty general Du Yu's description of his military campaign, where he likened enemy resistance to bamboo that splits upon meeting (迎) the blade (刃). Once the first joint splits, the rest follows easily (而解). The phrase emphasizes how problems, once properly approached, often resolve with surprising ease. It suggests finding the right approach or leverage point that makes seemingly difficult challenges straightforward. Modern usage describes problems that dissolve once the correct solution is applied.
chineseExample_tr: 一旦找到根本原因，問題就迎刃而解了。

example: Her indomitable spirit helped her overcome every obstacle.
chineseExample: 她不屈不挠的精神帮助她克服了每一个障碍。
description: This idiom combines two negations - not (不) bending (屈) and not (不) yielding (挠) - to describe absolute resistance to pressure. The phrase appears in historical texts praising martyrs and heroes who maintained their principles despite threats or torture. The double negation creates emphasis, suggesting complete imperviousness to coercion. It embodies the Confucian virtue of unwavering integrity and the Chinese cultural admiration for those who suffer rather than compromise. Modern usage praises determination and resilience in facing adversity.
description_tr: This idiom combines two negations - not (不) bending (屈) and not (不) yielding (撓) - to describe absolute resistance to pressure. The phrase appears in historical texts praising martyrs and heroes who maintained their principles despite threats or torture. The double negation creates emphasis, suggesting complete imperviousness to coercion. It embodies the Confucian virtue of unwavering integrity and the Chinese cultural admiration for those who suffer rather than compromise. Modern usage praises determination and resilience in facing adversity.
chineseExample_tr: 她不屈不撓的精神幫助她克服了每一個障礙。

example: The prosecution presented overwhelming evidence against the defendant.
chineseExample: 控方提出了铁证如山的证据。
description: This idiom describes evidence that is as solid as iron (铁) and as immovable as a mountain (如山). The combination of these two images emphasizes both the quality and quantity of proof - iron suggests irrefutability while mountain suggests overwhelming volume. The phrase emerged in legal and forensic contexts during the Ming Dynasty. It represents the ideal standard of proof that leaves no room for doubt. Modern usage commonly appears in legal, journalistic, and debate contexts to describe conclusive evidence.
description_tr: This idiom describes evidence that is as solid as iron (鐵) and as immovable as a mountain (如山). The combination of these two images emphasizes both the quality and quantity of proof - iron suggests irrefutability while mountain suggests overwhelming volume. The phrase emerged in legal and forensic contexts during the Ming Dynasty. It represents the ideal standard of proof that leaves no room for doubt. Modern usage commonly appears in legal, journalistic, and debate contexts to describe conclusive evidence.
chineseExample_tr: 控方提出了鐵證如山的證據。

example: The sculptor's work was so realistic it seemed ready to move.
chineseExample: 雕塑家的作品栩栩如生，仿佛随时会动起来。
description: This idiom uses the reduplicated 'xuxu' (栩栩), meaning vivid or lifelike, combined with 'as if alive' (如生) to describe art so realistic it appears living. The character 栩 originally referred to oak trees and their vibrant appearance. The phrase emerged from art criticism during the Han Dynasty, praising works that transcended mere representation to capture the essence of life. It embodies the Chinese aesthetic ideal of art that conveys spirit (神) rather than just form. Modern usage praises exceptionally realistic art, writing, or performance.
description_tr: This idiom uses the reduplicated 'xuxu' (栩栩), meaning vivid or lifelike, combined with 'as if alive' (如生) to describe art so realistic it appears living. The character 栩 originally referred to oak trees and their vibrant appearance. The phrase emerged from art criticism during the Han Dynasty, praising works that transcended mere representation to capture the essence of life. It embodies the Chinese aesthetic ideal of art that conveys spirit (神) rather than just form. Modern usage praises exceptionally realistic art, writing, or performance.
chineseExample_tr: 雕塑家的作品栩栩如生，仿佛隨時會動起來。

example: He fabricated the entire story out of thin air.
chineseExample: 他无中生有，编造了整个故事。
description: This idiom has dual origins. Philosophically, it derives from the Daoist concept in 'Tao Te Ching' that all things emerge from nothingness (无) to existence (有). Strategically, it appears in the 'Thirty-Six Stratagems' as a deceptive military tactic of creating threats or advantages from nothing. The phrase can describe both creative innovation and deceptive fabrication, depending on context. Modern usage often carries negative connotations of making false accusations or spreading rumors, though it can also describe genuine creativity or innovation.
description_tr: This idiom has dual origins. Philosophically, it derives from the Daoist concept in 'Tao Te Ching' that all things emerge from nothingness (無) to existence (有). Strategically, it appears in the 'Thirty-Six Stratagems' as a deceptive military tactic of creating threats or advantages from nothing. The phrase can describe both creative innovation and deceptive fabrication, depending on context. Modern usage often carries negative connotations of making false accusations or spreading rumors, though it can also describe genuine creativity or innovation.
chineseExample_tr: 他無中生有，編造了整個故事。

example: Before making a decision, we need to understand the full context.
chineseExample: 做决定之前，我们需要了解事情的来龙去脉。
description: This idiom originates from Chinese geomancy (feng shui), where 'dragon' (龙) refers to mountain ranges and 'veins' (脉) to their extensions. Geomancers traced the 'coming dragon' and 'going veins' to understand the landscape's energy flow. The phrase evolved to mean tracing the origins and developments of any matter - its causes, progression, and connections. It emphasizes the importance of understanding context and history. Modern usage describes investigating or explaining the full background and development of a situation or event.
metaphoric_meaning: The whole story; causes and effects
description_tr: This idiom originates from Chinese geomancy (feng shui), where 'dragon' (龍) refers to mountain ranges and 'veins' (脈) to their extensions. Geomancers traced the 'coming dragon' and 'going veins' to understand the landscape's energy flow. The phrase evolved to mean tracing the origins and developments of any matter - its causes, progression, and connections. It emphasizes the importance of understanding context and history. Modern usage describes investigating or explaining the full background and development of a situation or event.
chineseExample_tr: 做決定之前，我們需要了解事情的來龍去脈。

example: It's only natural that hard work leads to success.
chineseExample: 努力工作会带来成功，这是理所当然的。
description: This idiom combines 'according to reason' (理所) with 'naturally so' (当然) to express what logic and propriety dictate as obvious or appropriate. The concept of 'li' (理) in Neo-Confucian philosophy represents the rational principle underlying all things. The phrase suggests alignment between expectation and cosmic order. It appeared in Song Dynasty philosophical texts discussing moral imperatives. Modern usage can express either genuine natural logic or, sometimes critically, assumptions that may actually warrant questioning.
metaphoric_meaning: Naturally; as a matter of course
description_tr: This idiom combines 'according to reason' (理所) with 'naturally so' (當然) to express what logic and propriety dictate as obvious or appropriate. The concept of 'li' (理) in Neo-Confucian philosophy represents the rational principle underlying all things. The phrase suggests alignment between expectation and cosmic order. It appeared in Song Dynasty philosophical texts discussing moral imperatives. Modern usage can express either genuine natural logic or, sometimes critically, assumptions that may actually warrant questioning.
chineseExample_tr: 努力工作會帶來成功，這是理所當然的。

example: After losing the championship, the team left the field dejected.
chineseExample: 输掉冠军赛后，队员们垂头丧气地离开了球场。
description: This idiom describes the physical posture of dejection - head (头) drooping (垂) and spirit (气) lost (丧). The phrase captures how emotional states manifest in body language, reflecting the traditional Chinese understanding of mind-body unity. It appears in Tang Dynasty literature describing defeated warriors and disappointed scholars. The vivid physical imagery makes the emotional state instantly recognizable. Modern usage describes anyone in a state of discouragement, disappointment, or demoralization after failure or bad news.
description_tr: This idiom describes the physical posture of dejection - head (頭) drooping (垂) and spirit (氣) lost (喪). The phrase captures how emotional states manifest in body language, reflecting the traditional Chinese understanding of mind-body unity. It appears in Tang Dynasty literature describing defeated warriors and disappointed scholars. The vivid physical imagery makes the emotional state instantly recognizable. Modern usage describes anyone in a state of discouragement, disappointment, or demoralization after failure or bad news.
chineseExample_tr: 輸掉冠軍賽後，隊員們垂頭喪氣地離開了球場。

example: The plot twist was completely unexpected.
chineseExample: 剧情的反转完全意想不到。
description: This idiom describes something that one's thoughts (意想) could not (不) reach or anticipate (到). The phrase emphasizes the limits of imagination when confronted with surprising reality. It emerged in vernacular literature during the Ming Dynasty to describe plot twists and unexpected events. The structure suggests that what occurred was beyond the boundaries of what could reasonably be imagined. Modern usage commonly describes surprises, unexpected developments, or outcomes that defied all predictions.
description_tr: This idiom describes something that one's thoughts (意想) could not (不) reach or anticipate (到). The phrase emphasizes the limits of imagination when confronted with surprising reality. It emerged in vernacular literature during the Ming Dynasty to describe plot twists and unexpected events. The structure suggests that what occurred was beyond the boundaries of what could reasonably be imagined. Modern usage commonly describes surprises, unexpected developments, or outcomes that defied all predictions.
chineseExample_tr: 劇情的反轉完全意想不到。

example: We wish you smooth sailing in your new career.
chineseExample: 祝你在新的职业生涯中一帆风顺。
description: This idiom depicts a sailing vessel with one (一) sail (帆) catching favorable (顺) wind (风), moving smoothly toward its destination. It emerged from China's rich maritime history, particularly during the Song Dynasty when seafaring commerce flourished. The image of effortless progress powered by natural forces became a popular blessing for journeys and ventures. While literally about sea travel, it metaphorically wishes success without obstacles. Modern usage serves as a common blessing for new endeavors, graduations, and life transitions.
description_tr: This idiom depicts a sailing vessel with one (一) sail (帆) catching favorable (順) wind (風), moving smoothly toward its destination. It emerged from China's rich maritime history, particularly during the Song Dynasty when seafaring commerce flourished. The image of effortless progress powered by natural forces became a popular blessing for journeys and ventures. While literally about sea travel, it metaphorically wishes success without obstacles. Modern usage serves as a common blessing for new endeavors, graduations, and life transitions.
chineseExample_tr: 祝你在新的職業生涯中一帆風順。

example: He was caught in a dilemma, unable to please either side.
chineseExample: 他左右为难，无法让双方都满意。
description: This idiom describes being in difficulty (为难) whether going left (左) or right (右), trapped between two equally problematic choices. The spatial metaphor of being stuck between opposing directions vividly captures the experience of dilemmas. The phrase appeared in historical texts describing officials caught between conflicting loyalties or commanders facing impossible tactical situations. It embodies situations where every option carries significant costs. Modern usage describes any predicament where all available choices present serious difficulties.
metaphoric_meaning: In a dilemma; caught between difficulties
description_tr: This idiom describes being in difficulty (為難) whether going left (左) or right (右), trapped between two equally problematic choices. The spatial metaphor of being stuck between opposing directions vividly captures the experience of dilemmas. The phrase appeared in historical texts describing officials caught between conflicting loyalties or commanders facing impossible tactical situations. It embodies situations where every option carries significant costs. Modern usage describes any predicament where all available choices present serious difficulties.
chineseExample_tr: 他左右為難，無法讓雙方都滿意。

meaning: Think a hundred times still not understand
example: No matter how much she thought about it, she couldn't understand his decision.
chineseExample: 无论她怎么想，都对他的决定百思不解。
description: This idiom describes thinking (思) a hundred times (百) yet still not (不) understanding (解), expressing puzzlement that persists despite extensive contemplation. The number 'hundred' suggests numerous attempts rather than a literal count. The phrase emerged in literary contexts describing mysteries and paradoxes that resist rational explanation. It captures the frustration of encountering something genuinely incomprehensible. Modern usage describes confusion about decisions, behaviors, or events that defy logical explanation.
metaphoric_meaning: Unable to understand despite much thought
description_tr: This idiom describes thinking (思) a hundred times (百) yet still not (不) understanding (解), expressing puzzlement that persists despite extensive contemplation. The number 'hundred' suggests numerous attempts rather than a literal count. The phrase emerged in literary contexts describing mysteries and paradoxes that resist rational explanation. It captures the frustration of encountering something genuinely incomprehensible. Modern usage describes confusion about decisions, behaviors, or events that defy logical explanation.
chineseExample_tr: 無論她怎麼想，都對他的決定百思不解。

example: She volunteered to lead the difficult project.
chineseExample: 她自告奋勇，主动承担这个困难的项目。
description: This idiom describes volunteering (自告) with vigor (奋) and courage (勇), emphasizing initiative and bravery in stepping forward. The phrase combines self-nomination with the qualities needed to follow through. It appeared in historical texts describing brave soldiers or officials who volunteered for dangerous missions. The idiom values proactive contribution over passive waiting. Modern usage praises those who voluntarily take on challenges, especially difficult or risky ones that others avoid.
description_tr: This idiom describes volunteering (自告) with vigor (奮) and courage (勇), emphasizing initiative and bravery in stepping forward. The phrase combines self-nomination with the qualities needed to follow through. It appeared in historical texts describing brave soldiers or officials who volunteered for dangerous missions. The idiom values proactive contribution over passive waiting. Modern usage praises those who voluntarily take on challenges, especially difficult or risky ones that others avoid.
chineseExample_tr: 她自告奮勇，主動承擔這個困難的項目。

example: The army advanced rapidly, meeting little resistance.
chineseExample: 军队势如破竹，几乎没有遇到抵抗。
