Modern usage often celebrates individuality and uniqueness, though it can still describe being out of step with peers.
chineseExample_tr: 她獨特的風格使她與眾不同。

example: Despite his fame, the professor remains approachable to students.
chineseExample: 尽管名声很大，这位教授对学生仍然平易近人。
description: This idiom describes being level/even (平易) and easy to approach (近人). It praises those who, despite status or accomplishment, remain accessible and unintimidating to others. The phrase appeared in texts praising officials who didn't let position create distance. It values humility that maintains human connection across hierarchies. Modern usage commends accessible leaders, approachable experts, and anyone who makes others feel comfortable despite power differentials.
metaphoric_meaning: Approachable; easy to get along with
description_tr: This idiom describes being level/even (平易) and easy to approach (近人). It praises those who, despite status or accomplishment, remain accessible and unintimidating to others. The phrase appeared in texts praising officials who didn't let position create distance. It values humility that maintains human connection across hierarchies. Modern usage commends accessible leaders, approachable experts, and anyone who makes others feel comfortable despite power differentials.
chineseExample_tr: 儘管名聲很大，這位教授對學生仍然平易近人。

example: The discovery had a tremendous impact on the scientific community.
chineseExample: 这一发现在科学界引起了惊天动地的影响。
description: This idiom describes something so significant it startles (惊) heaven (天) and shakes (动) earth (地). The cosmic scale of the metaphor indicates events of tremendous magnitude. The phrase appeared in historical texts describing momentous battles, natural disasters, or political upheavals. It represents the highest degree of impact or importance. Modern usage describes groundbreaking achievements, major events, or anything with profound, far-reaching consequences.
description_tr: This idiom describes something so significant it startles (驚) heaven (天) and shakes (動) earth (地). The cosmic scale of the metaphor indicates events of tremendous magnitude. The phrase appeared in historical texts describing momentous battles, natural disasters, or political upheavals. It represents the highest degree of impact or importance. Modern usage describes groundbreaking achievements, major events, or anything with profound, far-reaching consequences.
chineseExample_tr: 這一發現在科學界引起了驚天動地的影響。

example: Without proper documentation, progress was impossible.
chineseExample: 没有适当的文件，工作寸步难行。
description: This idiom describes being unable to walk (难行) even an inch (寸步). The smallest unit of progress - a single step - becomes impossible. The phrase appeared in texts describing extreme difficulties or insurmountable obstacles. It captures the frustration of complete immobilization. Modern usage describes situations where progress is blocked entirely, whether by bureaucratic obstacles, resource constraints, or opposition that makes even minimal advancement impossible.
metaphoric_meaning: Unable to move forward at all
description_tr: This idiom describes being unable to walk (難行) even an inch (寸步). The smallest unit of progress - a single step - becomes impossible. The phrase appeared in texts describing extreme difficulties or insurmountable obstacles. It captures the frustration of complete immobilization. Modern usage describes situations where progress is blocked entirely, whether by bureaucratic obstacles, resource constraints, or opposition that makes even minimal advancement impossible.
chineseExample_tr: 沒有適當的文件，工作寸步難行。

example: She couldn't wait to share the good news with her family.
chineseExample: 她迫不及待地想和家人分享这个好消息。
description: This idiom describes urgency (迫) so great that one cannot (不及) wait (待). It captures the psychological state of intense anticipation or impatience. The phrase appeared in Ming Dynasty literature describing characters driven by time pressure or excitement. It represents the peak of eagerness where delay becomes intolerable. Modern usage describes intense anticipation, impatience to begin something, or excitement that cannot be contained.
description_tr: This idiom describes urgency (迫) so great that one cannot (不及) wait (待). It captures the psychological state of intense anticipation or impatience. The phrase appeared in Ming Dynasty literature describing characters driven by time pressure or excitement. It represents the peak of eagerness where delay becomes intolerable. Modern usage describes intense anticipation, impatience to begin something, or excitement that cannot be contained.
chineseExample_tr: 她迫不及待地想和家人分享這個好消息。

meaning: Orderly like a well with sequence
example: The files were arranged in perfect order.
chineseExample: 文件排列得井然有序。
description: This idiom describes orderliness like a well (井然) with proper sequence (有序). The well metaphor relates to the grid pattern of ancient Chinese field systems where wells marked intersections. This regular pattern represented ideal organization. The phrase appeared in texts praising well-administered territories. Modern usage describes anything arranged systematically and neatly, from physical spaces to administrative systems to personal schedules.
description_tr: This idiom describes orderliness like a well (井然) with proper sequence (有序). The well metaphor relates to the grid pattern of ancient Chinese field systems where wells marked intersections. This regular pattern represented ideal organization. The phrase appeared in texts praising well-administered territories. Modern usage describes anything arranged systematically and neatly, from physical spaces to administrative systems to personal schedules.
chineseExample_tr: 文件排列得井然有序。

example: After years of struggle, success finally arrived.
chineseExample: 经过多年奋斗，终于苦尽甘来。
description: This idiom describes bitterness (苦) ending (尽) and sweetness (甘) arriving (来). It offers hope that suffering is temporary and will be followed by better times. The phrase appeared in texts encouraging perseverance through hardship. It reflects the cyclical Chinese understanding of fortune where extremes eventually reverse. Modern usage consoles those in difficulty with the promise that persistence will eventually be rewarded with success or happiness.
description_tr: This idiom describes bitterness (苦) ending (盡) and sweetness (甘) arriving (來). It offers hope that suffering is temporary and will be followed by better times. The phrase appeared in texts encouraging perseverance through hardship. It reflects the cyclical Chinese understanding of fortune where extremes eventually reverse. Modern usage consoles those in difficulty with the promise that persistence will eventually be rewarded with success or happiness.
chineseExample_tr: 經過多年奮鬥，終於苦盡甘來。

example: The sudden question left him completely flustered.
chineseExample: 这个突如其来的问题让他手足无措。
description: This idiom describes hands (手) and feet (足) having no place (无措) to go - not knowing where to put one's limbs. The physical awkwardness represents mental confusion and panic. The phrase appeared in Confucian texts describing the discomfort of impropriety. It captures the paralysis of sudden confusion where even basic physical coordination fails. Modern usage describes being so flustered or surprised that one doesn't know how to react.
description_tr: This idiom describes hands (手) and feet (足) having no place (無措) to go - not knowing where to put one's limbs. The physical awkwardness represents mental confusion and panic. The phrase appeared in Confucian texts describing the discomfort of impropriety. It captures the paralysis of sudden confusion where even basic physical coordination fails. Modern usage describes being so flustered or surprised that one doesn't know how to react.
chineseExample_tr: 這個突如其來的問題讓他手足無措。

example: His arrogance made him treat everyone with contempt.
chineseExample: 他的傲慢让他目中无人。
description: This idiom describes having no (无) person (人) in one's eyes (目中) - looking through people as if they don't exist. It criticizes extreme arrogance that refuses to acknowledge others. The phrase appeared in texts condemning proud officials who treated inferiors with contempt. It represents a failure of basic human respect. Modern usage criticizes those who act superior and disregard others, often due to wealth, status, or achievements that inflate their ego.
metaphoric_meaning: Extremely arrogant; look down on everyone
description_tr: This idiom describes having no (無) person (人) in one's eyes (目中) - looking through people as if they don't exist. It criticizes extreme arrogance that refuses to acknowledge others. The phrase appeared in texts condemning proud officials who treated inferiors with contempt. It represents a failure of basic human respect. Modern usage criticizes those who act superior and disregard others, often due to wealth, status, or achievements that inflate their ego.
chineseExample_tr: 他的傲慢讓他目中無人。

example: The local economy is thriving with new businesses.
chineseExample: 当地经济欣欣向荣，新企业不断涌现。
description: This idiom uses the reduplicated 'xinxin' (欣欣), meaning flourishing or joyful growth, combined with heading toward (向) prosperity (荣). It originally described vigorous plant growth and evolved to describe any thriving development. The phrase appeared in texts celebrating agricultural abundance and economic prosperity. Modern usage describes flourishing businesses, growing economies, thriving communities, or any situation characterized by vigorous positive development.
description_tr: This idiom uses the reduplicated 'xinxin' (欣欣), meaning flourishing or joyful growth, combined with heading toward (向) prosperity (榮). It originally described vigorous plant growth and evolved to describe any thriving development. The phrase appeared in texts celebrating agricultural abundance and economic prosperity. Modern usage describes flourishing businesses, growing economies, thriving communities, or any situation characterized by vigorous positive development.
chineseExample_tr: 當地經濟欣欣向榮，新企業不斷湧現。

example: After repeated offenses, his patience finally ran out.
chineseExample: 在反复冒犯后，他终于忍无可忍。
description: This idiom describes enduring (忍) until there is nothing (无) left that can (可) be endured (忍). The repetition of 'ren' (忍, endure) emphasizes the limit being reached. Chinese culture values patience and forbearance, making this breaking point significant. The phrase appeared in texts describing moments when even the most patient person must act. Modern usage marks the threshold where tolerance ends and response becomes necessary.
metaphoric_meaning: Beyond endurance; can't take it anymore
description_tr: This idiom describes enduring (忍) until there is nothing (無) left that can (可) be endured (忍). The repetition of 'ren' (忍, endure) emphasizes the limit being reached. Chinese culture values patience and forbearance, making this breaking point significant. The phrase appeared in texts describing moments when even the most patient person must act. Modern usage marks the threshold where tolerance ends and response becomes necessary.
chineseExample_tr: 在反復冒犯後，他終於忍無可忍。

example: The commander made a swift decision in the crisis.
chineseExample: 指挥官在危机中当机立断。
description: This idiom describes facing (当) an opportunity or moment (机) and immediately (立) deciding (断). It praises decisive action that seizes the moment without hesitation. The phrase originated in military strategy texts emphasizing the importance of timely decisions. Hesitation in critical moments can be fatal. Modern usage praises quick, decisive action in time-sensitive situations, particularly in leadership, crisis management, and competitive environments.
description_tr: This idiom describes facing (當) an opportunity or moment (機) and immediately (立) deciding (斷). It praises decisive action that seizes the moment without hesitation. The phrase originated in military strategy texts emphasizing the importance of timely decisions. Hesitation in critical moments can be fatal. Modern usage praises quick, decisive action in time-sensitive situations, particularly in leadership, crisis management, and competitive environments.
chineseExample_tr: 指揮官在危機中當機立斷。

example: The devastation from the earthquake was deeply shocking.
chineseExample: 地震造成的破坏触目惊心。
description: This idiom describes sights that touch (触) the eyes (目) and shock (惊) the heart (心). It captures the visceral impact of disturbing scenes that affect both vision and emotion. The phrase appeared in texts describing disasters, battlefields, and scenes of suffering. It emphasizes how some sights penetrate beyond mere observation to deeply disturb the viewer. Modern usage describes shocking scenes, alarming statistics, or disturbing evidence that profoundly affects observers.
description_tr: This idiom describes sights that touch (觸) the eyes (目) and shock (驚) the heart (心). It captures the visceral impact of disturbing scenes that affect both vision and emotion. The phrase appeared in texts describing disasters, battlefields, and scenes of suffering. It emphasizes how some sights penetrate beyond mere observation to deeply disturb the viewer. Modern usage describes shocking scenes, alarming statistics, or disturbing evidence that profoundly affects observers.
chineseExample_tr: 地震造成的破壞觸目驚心。

example: After his explanation, everything suddenly became clear.
chineseExample: 经过他的解释，一切豁然开朗。
description: This idiom describes something suddenly (豁然) opening up (开) into brightness (朗). It comes from Tao Yuanming's famous 'Peach Blossom Spring,' where travelers emerged from a dark cave into a bright paradise. The phrase captures the moment of sudden clarity or understanding after confusion. Modern usage describes breakthrough moments of comprehension, when previously confusing matters suddenly become clear, or when new perspectives illuminate problems.
metaphoric_meaning: Suddenly see the light; become clear
description_tr: This idiom describes something suddenly (豁然) opening up (開) into brightness (朗). It comes from Tao Yuanming's famous 'Peach Blossom Spring,' where travelers emerged from a dark cave into a bright paradise. The phrase captures the moment of sudden clarity or understanding after confusion. Modern usage describes breakthrough moments of comprehension, when previously confusing matters suddenly become clear, or when new perspectives illuminate problems.
chineseExample_tr: 經過他的解釋，一切豁然開朗。

example: The public exposure left him feeling deeply ashamed.
chineseExample: 公开曝光让他无地自容。
description: This idiom describes having no (无) ground (地) to contain (容) oneself (自) - wishing to disappear from shame. The image of having nowhere to hide captures extreme embarrassment. The phrase appeared in texts describing disgrace so profound that the person wishes the earth would swallow them. Modern usage describes intense shame or embarrassment where one wants to disappear, often from public humiliation or exposure of wrongdoing.
description_tr: This idiom describes having no (無) ground (地) to contain (容) oneself (自) - wishing to disappear from shame. The image of having nowhere to hide captures extreme embarrassment. The phrase appeared in texts describing disgrace so profound that the person wishes the earth would swallow them. Modern usage describes intense shame or embarrassment where one wants to disappear, often from public humiliation or exposure of wrongdoing.
chineseExample_tr: 公開曝光讓他無地自容。

example: The busy street sees constant traffic day and night.
chineseExample: 这条繁忙的街道车流川流不息。
description: This idiom describes rivers (川) flowing (流) without (不) ceasing (息). The natural image of perpetually flowing water represents continuous movement or activity. The phrase appeared in classical texts describing busy markets and prosperous cities. It captures the vitality of constant activity. Modern usage commonly describes heavy traffic, busy streets, or any situation characterized by continuous, uninterrupted flow of people, vehicles, or activity.
description_tr: This idiom describes rivers (川) flowing (流) without (不) ceasing (息). The natural image of perpetually flowing water represents continuous movement or activity. The phrase appeared in classical texts describing busy markets and prosperous cities. It captures the vitality of constant activity. Modern usage commonly describes heavy traffic, busy streets, or any situation characterized by continuous, uninterrupted flow of people, vehicles, or activity.
chineseExample_tr: 這條繁忙的街道車流川流不息。

meaning: Dwelling in peace think of danger
example: Even during prosperous times, wise leaders prepare for potential crises.
chineseExample: 即使在繁荣时期，明智的领导者也会居安思危。
description: This idiom advises thinking of (思) danger (危) while dwelling in (居) peace (安). It comes from 'Zuozhuan,' an ancient Chinese chronicle, warning against complacency during good times. The phrase embodies the wisdom of preparation and foresight. Chinese history provided many examples of kingdoms that fell because they grew complacent. Modern usage encourages vigilance and contingency planning even when circumstances seem favorable.
metaphoric_meaning: Be prepared for danger in times of safety
description_tr: This idiom advises thinking of (思) danger (危) while dwelling in (居) peace (安). It comes from 'Zuozhuan,' an ancient Chinese chronicle, warning against complacency during good times. The phrase embodies the wisdom of preparation and foresight. Chinese history provided many examples of kingdoms that fell because they grew complacent. Modern usage encourages vigilance and contingency planning even when circumstances seem favorable.
chineseExample_tr: 即使在繁榮時期，明智的領導者也會居安思危。

example: Technology is advancing at an unprecedented pace.
chineseExample: 科技发展日新月异。
description: This idiom describes being new (新) every day (日) and different (异) every month (月). It captures rapid, continuous change and progress. The phrase echoes the Confucian classic 'Great Learning,' which advocates constant self-renewal. Originally about moral cultivation, it evolved to describe any field experiencing rapid development. Modern usage commonly describes technological advancement, economic growth, or any area of fast-paced change and improvement.
description_tr: This idiom describes being new (新) every day (日) and different (異) every month (月). It captures rapid, continuous change and progress. The phrase echoes the Confucian classic 'Great Learning,' which advocates constant self-renewal. Originally about moral cultivation, it evolved to describe any field experiencing rapid development. Modern usage commonly describes technological advancement, economic growth, or any area of fast-paced change and improvement.
chineseExample_tr: 科技發展日新月異。

example: The virtual reality experience made users feel like they were really there.
chineseExample: 虚拟现实体验让用户身临其境。
description: This idiom describes one's body (身) arriving at (临) that (其) situation or place (境). It emphasizes experiential presence rather than remote observation. The phrase appeared in texts distinguishing direct experience from secondhand knowledge. It values immersive understanding that comes from personal involvement. Modern usage describes immersive experiences, realistic simulations, or vivid descriptions that transport the audience to feel present in another scene or situation.
description_tr: This idiom describes one's body (身) arriving at (臨) that (其) situation or place (境). It emphasizes experiential presence rather than remote observation. The phrase appeared in texts distinguishing direct experience from secondhand knowledge. It values immersive understanding that comes from personal involvement. Modern usage describes immersive experiences, realistic simulations, or vivid descriptions that transport the audience to feel present in another scene or situation.
chineseExample_tr: 虛擬現實體驗讓用戶身臨其境。

example: The audience fell completely silent when the speaker began.
chineseExample: 演讲者开始时，观众鸦雀无声。
description: This idiom describes a scene where even crows (鸦) and sparrows (雀) make no (无) sound (声). These common birds are normally noisy, so their silence indicates extreme quiet. The phrase appeared in texts describing solemn occasions, tense moments, or places of deep concentration. It represents perfect silence where even ambient noise disappears. Modern usage describes complete silence in normally noisy settings, often indicating attention, tension, or solemnity.
metaphoric_meaning: Completely silent; so quiet you could hear a pin drop
description_tr: This idiom describes a scene where even crows (鴉) and sparrows (雀) make no (無) sound (聲). These common birds are normally noisy, so their silence indicates extreme quiet. The phrase appeared in texts describing solemn occasions, tense moments, or places of deep concentration. It represents perfect silence where even ambient noise disappears. Modern usage describes complete silence in normally noisy settings, often indicating attention, tension, or solemnity.
chineseExample_tr: 演講者開始時，觀眾鴉雀無聲。

example: The project proceeded methodically through each phase.
chineseExample: 项目按部就班地进行每个阶段。
description: This idiom describes following (按就) proper departments (部) and classes or sequences (班). It emphasizes methodical procedure following established order. The phrase originated in bureaucratic contexts describing proper administrative process. It values systematic approach over improvisation. Modern usage describes doing things step by step according to established procedures, sometimes positively (thorough) and sometimes negatively (inflexible or slow).
metaphoric_meaning: Follow established procedures; step by step
description_tr: This idiom describes following (按就) proper departments (部) and classes or sequences (班). It emphasizes methodical procedure following established order. The phrase originated in bureaucratic contexts describing proper administrative process. It values systematic approach over improvisation. Modern usage describes doing things step by step according to established procedures, sometimes positively (thorough) and sometimes negatively (inflexible or slow).
chineseExample_tr: 項目按部就班地進行每個階段。

example: They were so busy they couldn't handle everything.
chineseExample: 他们忙得不可开交。
description: This idiom describes being unable (不可) to open or separate (开交) - being so entangled in activity that one cannot extricate oneself. Originally describing situations of intense conflict where parties couldn't disengage, it evolved to describe being overwhelmingly busy. The phrase appeared in texts describing fierce battles or heated disputes. Modern usage commonly describes being extremely busy, deeply embroiled in conflict, or intensely occupied with demanding tasks.
description_tr: This idiom describes being unable (不可) to open or separate (開交) - being so entangled in activity that one cannot extricate oneself. Originally describing situations of intense conflict where parties couldn't disengage, it evolved to describe being overwhelmingly busy. The phrase appeared in texts describing fierce battles or heated disputes. Modern usage commonly describes being extremely busy, deeply embroiled in conflict, or intensely occupied with demanding tasks.
chineseExample_tr: 他們忙得不可開交。

example: Instead of competing directly, she found an innovative alternative approach.
chineseExample: 她没有直接竞争，而是另辟蹊径。
description: This idiom describes separately (另) opening (辟) a side path (蹊径) - finding an alternative route. It praises creative problem-solving that avoids direct confrontation with obstacles by discovering new approaches. The phrase appeared in texts praising innovative thinkers who found unexpected solutions. Modern usage celebrates creative thinking, unconventional strategies, and innovative approaches that succeed by departing from established methods.
metaphoric_meaning: Find an alternative approach; think outside the box
description_tr: This idiom describes separately (另) opening (闢) a side path (蹊徑) - finding an alternative route. It praises creative problem-solving that avoids direct confrontation with obstacles by discovering new approaches. The phrase appeared in texts praising innovative thinkers who found unexpected solutions. Modern usage celebrates creative thinking, unconventional strategies, and innovative approaches that succeed by departing from established methods.
chineseExample_tr: 她沒有直接競爭，而是另闢蹊徑。

example: The couple understood each other perfectly without words.
chineseExample: 这对夫妻心心相印，无需言语。
description: This idiom describes hearts (心心) mutually (相) sealing or imprinting upon (印) each other. It comes from Chan (Zen) Buddhism describing the wordless transmission of understanding between master and disciple. The phrase evolved to describe perfect mutual understanding in any close relationship. Modern usage describes deep rapport where communication transcends words, often in romantic partnerships, close friendships, or mentor relationships.
metaphoric_meaning: Perfect mutual understanding; hearts in harmony
description_tr: This idiom describes hearts (心心) mutually (相) sealing or imprinting upon (印) each other. It comes from Chan (Zen) Buddhism describing the wordless transmission of understanding between master and disciple. The phrase evolved to describe perfect mutual understanding in any close relationship. Modern usage describes deep rapport where communication transcends words, often in romantic partnerships, close friendships, or mentor relationships.
chineseExample_tr: 這對夫妻心心相印，無需言語。

example: With careful budgeting, they managed to save enough for the house.
chineseExample: 通过精打细算，他们存够了买房的钱。
description: This idiom describes precisely (精) calculating (打) and carefully (细) accounting (算). It emphasizes careful resource management through detailed planning. The phrase appeared in texts praising frugal household management. It represents the virtue of economic prudence through attention to detail. Modern usage describes careful budgeting, efficient resource allocation, or meticulous financial planning that maximizes value from limited resources.
metaphoric_meaning: Calculate carefully; be frugal and precise
description_tr: This idiom describes precisely (精) calculating (打) and carefully (細) accounting (算). It emphasizes careful resource management through detailed planning. The phrase appeared in texts praising frugal household management. It represents the virtue of economic prudence through attention to detail. Modern usage describes careful budgeting, efficient resource allocation, or meticulous financial planning that maximizes value from limited resources.
chineseExample_tr: 通過精打細算，他們存夠了買房的錢。

example: The research team returned with abundant findings.
chineseExample: 研究团队满载而归，收获颇丰。
description: This idiom describes returning (归) fully (满) loaded (载). Originally describing fishing boats or trading vessels returning with holds full of catch or goods, it evolved to describe any successful venture that yields abundant results. The phrase appeared in texts celebrating successful expeditions and harvests. Modern usage describes returning from any endeavor with significant gains, whether material goods, knowledge, or achievements.
description_tr: This idiom describes returning (歸) fully (滿) loaded (載). Originally describing fishing boats or trading vessels returning with holds full of catch or goods, it evolved to describe any successful venture that yields abundant results. The phrase appeared in texts celebrating successful expeditions and harvests. Modern usage describes returning from any endeavor with significant gains, whether material goods, knowledge, or achievements.
chineseExample_tr: 研究團隊滿載而歸，收穫頗豐。

meaning: Meeting each other regret it was late
example: The two researchers wished they had met years earlier.
chineseExample: 两位研究者相见恨晚，希望能更早相识。
description: This idiom describes meeting (相见) and regretting (恨) it was late (晚). It expresses the feeling when connecting with someone special and wishing the relationship had begun sooner. The phrase appeared in classical literature describing profound connections between people who felt their meeting was overdue. Modern usage expresses strong affinity with someone new, suggesting the relationship feels as if it should have started long ago.
metaphoric_meaning: Regret not meeting sooner; instant rapport
description_tr: This idiom describes meeting (相見) and regretting (恨) it was late (晚). It expresses the feeling when connecting with someone special and wishing the relationship had begun sooner. The phrase appeared in classical literature describing profound connections between people who felt their meeting was overdue. Modern usage expresses strong affinity with someone new, suggesting the relationship feels as if it should have started long ago.
chineseExample_tr: 兩位研究者相見恨晚，希望能更早相識。

example: His flimsy argument fell apart without anyone even challenging it.
chineseExample: 他站不住脚的论点不攻自破。
description: This idiom describes something collapsing (破) by itself (自) without (不) attack (攻). Originally a military term for fortifications so weak they crumble without assault, it evolved to describe arguments or positions that are inherently unsustainable. The phrase appeared in strategy texts discussing situations where patience defeats opponents naturally. Modern usage describes weak arguments, flawed plans, or unstable situations that fail without external intervention.
description_tr: This idiom describes something collapsing (破) by itself (自) without (不) attack (攻). Originally a military term for fortifications so weak they crumble without assault, it evolved to describe arguments or positions that are inherently unsustainable. The phrase appeared in strategy texts discussing situations where patience defeats opponents naturally. Modern usage describes weak arguments, flawed plans, or unstable situations that fail without external intervention.
chineseExample_tr: 他站不住腳的論點不攻自破。

example: Having experienced similar losses, they truly understood each other.
chineseExample: 经历过类似的失去，他们同病相怜。
description: This idiom describes those with the same (同) illness or affliction (病) mutually (相) sympathizing (怜). It captures the special understanding between people who share similar suffering. The phrase originated in Han Dynasty texts describing exiles who found comfort in shared misfortune. Modern usage describes solidarity born from common hardship, where shared experience creates deeper understanding and compassion than outsiders can offer.
metaphoric_meaning: Sympathize with those sharing similar troubles
description_tr: This idiom describes those with the same (同) illness or affliction (病) mutually (相) sympathizing (憐). It captures the special understanding between people who share similar suffering. The phrase originated in Han Dynasty texts describing exiles who found comfort in shared misfortune. Modern usage describes solidarity born from common hardship, where shared experience creates deeper understanding and compassion than outsiders can offer.
chineseExample_tr: 經歷過類似的失去，他們同病相憐。

example: Assigning a senior engineer to simple data entry is a waste of talent.
chineseExample: 让高级工程师做简单的数据录入是大材小用。
description: This idiom describes big (大) talent (材) being given small (小) use (用) - wasting capable people on trivial tasks. The phrase criticizes poor resource allocation that underutilizes valuable abilities. It appeared in texts discussing governance and personnel management. The idiom values matching abilities to appropriate challenges. Modern usage describes situations where qualified individuals are assigned tasks far below their capabilities, wasting both their potential and organizational resources.
description_tr: This idiom describes big (大) talent (材) being given small (小) use (用) - wasting capable people on trivial tasks. The phrase criticizes poor resource allocation that underutilizes valuable abilities. It appeared in texts discussing governance and personnel management. The idiom values matching abilities to appropriate challenges. Modern usage describes situations where qualified individuals are assigned tasks far below their capabilities, wasting both their potential and organizational resources.
chineseExample_tr: 讓高級工程師做簡單的數據錄入是大材小用。

example: His contribution to the project was negligible.
chineseExample: 他对项目的贡献微不足道。
description: This idiom describes something so tiny (微) that it's not (不) worth (足) mentioning (道). It emphasizes extreme insignificance or negligibility. The phrase appeared in texts using self-deprecation or describing genuinely minor matters. It can express humility when self-referential or dismissal when describing others' contributions. Modern usage describes things so small or unimportant they don't merit attention, from minor inconveniences to trivial contributions.
description_tr: This idiom describes something so tiny (微) that it's not (不) worth (足) mentioning (道). It emphasizes extreme insignificance or negligibility. The phrase appeared in texts using self-deprecation or describing genuinely minor matters. It can express humility when self-referential or dismissal when describing others' contributions. Modern usage describes things so small or unimportant they don't merit attention, from minor inconveniences to trivial contributions.
chineseExample_tr: 他對項目的貢獻微不足道。

example: She always practices what she preaches.
chineseExample: 她总是言行一致。
description: This idiom describes words (言) and actions (行) being consistent (一致). It embodies the Confucian ideal of integrity where speech matches behavior. The phrase appeared in texts praising individuals whose deeds fulfilled their promises. Confucius emphasized that the superior person is slow to speak but quick to act, and their words must match their actions. Modern usage praises those who practice what they preach and maintain consistency between stated values and actual behavior.
description_tr: This idiom describes words (言) and actions (行) being consistent (一致). It embodies the Confucian ideal of integrity where speech matches behavior. The phrase appeared in texts praising individuals whose deeds fulfilled their promises. Confucius emphasized that the superior person is slow to speak but quick to act, and their words must match their actions. Modern usage praises those who practice what they preach and maintain consistency between stated values and actual behavior.
chineseExample_tr: 她總是言行一致。

example: Don't underestimate yourself - you have great potential.
chineseExample: 不要妄自菲薄，你有很大的潜力。
description: This idiom describes wrongly (妄) depreciating (菲薄) oneself (自). It warns against excessive self-deprecation that undervalues one's abilities. The phrase comes from Zhuge Liang's famous memorial 'Chu Shi Biao,' where he urged the young emperor not to underestimate himself. It distinguishes harmful self-doubt from healthy humility. Modern usage encourages people to recognize their worth and capabilities, counseling against excessive modesty that undermines confidence and potential.
description_tr: This idiom describes wrongly (妄) depreciating (菲薄) oneself (自). It warns against excessive self-deprecation that undervalues one's abilities. The phrase comes from Zhuge Liang's famous memorial 'Chu Shi Biao,' where he urged the young emperor not to underestimate himself. It distinguishes harmful self-doubt from healthy humility. Modern usage encourages people to recognize their worth and capabilities, counseling against excessive modesty that undermines confidence and potential.
chineseExample_tr: 不要妄自菲薄，你有很大的潛力。

example: Cycling to work saves money and improves health at the same time.
chineseExample: 骑车上班既省钱又健身，一举两得。
description: This idiom describes one (一) action (举) yielding two (两) gains (得). It celebrates efficiency that achieves multiple objectives simultaneously. The phrase appeared in strategy texts praising tactics that accomplish several goals at once. It represents the ideal of maximizing returns from single efforts. Modern usage describes any action, decision, or approach that achieves multiple benefits from a single investment of time, money, or effort.
metaphoric_meaning: Kill two birds with one stone
description_tr: This idiom describes one (一) action (舉) yielding two (兩) gains (得). It celebrates efficiency that achieves multiple objectives simultaneously. The phrase appeared in strategy texts praising tactics that accomplish several goals at once. It represents the ideal of maximizing returns from single efforts. Modern usage describes any action, decision, or approach that achieves multiple benefits from a single investment of time, money, or effort.
chineseExample_tr: 騎車上班既省錢又健身，一舉兩得。

example: He couldn't help but smile when he saw her.
chineseExample: 看到她，他不由自主地笑了。
description: This idiom describes actions not (不) controlled (由自主) by one's own will. It captures involuntary responses where emotions or instincts override conscious control. The phrase appeared in texts describing overwhelming emotional states. It acknowledges that some responses transcend deliberate choice. Modern usage describes involuntary reactions - laughing, crying, or other behaviors that occur spontaneously without conscious decision.
description_tr: This idiom describes actions not (不) controlled (由自主) by one's own will. It captures involuntary responses where emotions or instincts override conscious control. The phrase appeared in texts describing overwhelming emotional states. It acknowledges that some responses transcend deliberate choice. Modern usage describes involuntary reactions - laughing, crying, or other behaviors that occur spontaneously without conscious decision.
chineseExample_tr: 看到她，他不由自主地笑了。

meaning: Hundred times nothing to rely on
example: With nothing to do all day, she felt utterly bored.
chineseExample: 整天无所事事，她百无聊赖。
description: This idiom describes having a hundred (百) times nothing (无) to depend on or occupy (聊赖). It captures extreme boredom and listlessness when one has nothing meaningful to engage with. The phrase appeared in texts describing idle officials or those in forced retirement. It represents not just boredom but a deeper sense of purposelessness. Modern usage describes profound boredom, aimlessness, or the melancholy of having nothing to occupy one's time meaningfully.
description_tr: This idiom describes having a hundred (百) times nothing (無) to depend on or occupy (聊賴). It captures extreme boredom and listlessness when one has nothing meaningful to engage with. The phrase appeared in texts describing idle officials or those in forced retirement. It represents not just boredom but a deeper sense of purposelessness. Modern usage describes profound boredom, aimlessness, or the melancholy of having nothing to occupy one's time meaningfully.
chineseExample_tr: 整天無所事事，她百無聊賴。

meaning: Go into boiling water step on fire
example: He would go through fire and water for his family.
chineseExample: 为了家人，他愿意赴汤蹈火。
description: This idiom describes going into (赴) boiling water (汤) and stepping on (蹈) fire (火). It represents willingness to face extreme danger or hardship. The phrase appeared in ancient texts describing loyal subjects who would risk death for their lords. It embodies the highest form of commitment and courage. Modern usage expresses willingness to make extreme sacrifices for important causes, loved ones, or deeply held beliefs.
metaphoric_meaning: Go through fire and water; brave any danger
description_tr: This idiom describes going into (赴) boiling water (湯) and stepping on (蹈) fire (火). It represents willingness to face extreme danger or hardship. The phrase appeared in ancient texts describing loyal subjects who would risk death for their lords. It embodies the highest form of commitment and courage. Modern usage expresses willingness to make extreme sacrifices for important causes, loved ones, or deeply held beliefs.
chineseExample_tr: 為了家人，他願意赴湯蹈火。

meaning: Tip of awl emerges from bag
example: Among hundreds of applicants, she stood out from the crowd.
chineseExample: 在数百名申请者中，她脱颖而出。
description: This idiom originates from a story where the advisor Mao Sui described talent like an awl in a bag - if truly sharp, its tip (颖) will emerge (脱而出). The phrase describes exceptional ability that becomes visible despite obscurity. It appeared in 'Records of the Grand Historian' praising those whose merit cannot be hidden. Modern usage describes standing out from a group, particularly through demonstrated excellence that distinguishes one from competitors.
description_tr: This idiom originates from a story where the advisor Mao Sui described talent like an awl in a bag - if truly sharp, its tip (穎) will emerge (脫而出). The phrase describes exceptional ability that becomes visible despite obscurity. It appeared in 'Records of the Grand Historian' praising those whose merit cannot be hidden. Modern usage describes standing out from a group, particularly through demonstrated excellence that distinguishes one from competitors.
chineseExample_tr: 在數百名申請者中，她脫穎而出。

example: His innate sense of compassion moved him to help the stranger.
chineseExample: 他的恻隐之心促使他帮助陌生人。
description: This idiom describes the heart (心) of compassion (恻隐). It comes from Mencius, who argued this feeling of pain at others' suffering is innate to all humans, proving human nature is fundamentally good. The phrase represents the instinctive sympathy that arises when witnessing others' distress. Modern usage describes natural compassion and empathy, the moral intuition that responds to suffering and motivates benevolent action.
description_tr: This idiom describes the heart (心) of compassion (惻隱). It comes from Mencius, who argued this feeling of pain at others' suffering is innate to all humans, proving human nature is fundamentally good. The phrase represents the instinctive sympathy that arises when witnessing others' distress. Modern usage describes natural compassion and empathy, the moral intuition that responds to suffering and motivates benevolent action.
chineseExample_tr: 他的惻隱之心促使他幫助陌生人。

example: The chart makes the data immediately understandable.
chineseExample: 这张图表让数据一目了然。
description: This idiom describes becoming completely clear (了然) with one (一) look (目). It praises clarity that enables instant comprehension without explanation. The phrase appeared in texts praising well-organized information or transparent situations. It values effective communication and presentation. Modern usage describes anything designed for immediate understanding - from data visualizations to well-structured explanations to situations that are obviously clear.
description_tr: This idiom describes becoming completely clear (了然) with one (一) look (目). It praises clarity that enables instant comprehension without explanation. The phrase appeared in texts praising well-organized information or transparent situations. It values effective communication and presentation. Modern usage describes anything designed for immediate understanding - from data visualizations to well-structured explanations to situations that are obviously clear.
chineseExample_tr: 這張圖表讓數據一目了然。

example: His belief that she would agree was just wishful thinking.
chineseExample: 他认为她会同意，不过是一厢情愿。
description: This idiom describes feelings or wishes (情愿) from only one side (一厢). It depicts expectations that exist only in one party's mind without reciprocation or basis in reality. The phrase appeared in texts describing unrequited feelings and unfounded assumptions. It warns against projecting one's desires onto situations without evidence. Modern usage describes wishful thinking, one-sided assumptions, or expectations not grounded in mutual agreement or reality.
description_tr: This idiom describes feelings or wishes (情願) from only one side (一廂). It depicts expectations that exist only in one party's mind without reciprocation or basis in reality. The phrase appeared in texts describing unrequited feelings and unfounded assumptions. It warns against projecting one's desires onto situations without evidence. Modern usage describes wishful thinking, one-sided assumptions, or expectations not grounded in mutual agreement or reality.
chineseExample_tr: 他認為她會同意，不過是一廂情願。

example: As the whistleblower, she became the target of everyone's criticism.
chineseExample: 作为举报人，她成了众矢之的。
description: This idiom describes being the target (的) of many (众) arrows (矢). It depicts someone who attracts criticism or attack from multiple directions simultaneously. The phrase appeared in texts describing those who became focal points of collective hostility. It captures the vulnerability of being singled out for group attack. Modern usage describes anyone who becomes the focus of widespread criticism, blame, or opposition from multiple sources.
description_tr: This idiom describes being the target (的) of many (眾) arrows (矢). It depicts someone who attracts criticism or attack from multiple directions simultaneously. The phrase appeared in texts describing those who became focal points of collective hostility. It captures the vulnerability of being singled out for group attack. Modern usage describes anyone who becomes the focus of widespread criticism, blame, or opposition from multiple sources.
chineseExample_tr: 作為舉報人，她成了眾矢之的。

example: The new funding will greatly strengthen our capabilities.
chineseExample: 新的资金将使我们如虎添翼。
description: This idiom describes being like a tiger (如虎) that adds (添) wings (翼). A tiger is already formidable; with wings, it becomes unstoppable. The phrase describes adding capabilities to something already powerful. It appeared in texts describing reinforcements to already strong positions. Modern usage describes gaining additional advantages that multiply existing strengths, making an already capable entity much more powerful.
metaphoric_meaning: Add to one's strength; become even more powerful
description_tr: This idiom describes being like a tiger (如虎) that adds (添) wings (翼). A tiger is already formidable; with wings, it becomes unstoppable. The phrase describes adding capabilities to something already powerful. It appeared in texts describing reinforcements to already strong positions. Modern usage describes gaining additional advantages that multiply existing strengths, making an already capable entity much more powerful.
chineseExample_tr: 新的資金將使我們如虎添翼。

example: His superficial reading left him with only a shallow understanding.
chineseExample: 他浅尝辄止的阅读让他不求甚解。
description: This idiom describes not (不) seeking (求) thorough (甚) understanding (解). It originated from Tao Yuanming's essay where he described his reading style as enjoying books without demanding deep understanding of every detail. This can be positive (appreciating the gestalt) or negative (superficiality). Modern usage often criticizes shallow engagement with material, though it sometimes positively describes a relaxed, enjoyment-focused approach to reading.
description_tr: This idiom describes not (不) seeking (求) thorough (甚) understanding (解). It originated from Tao Yuanming's essay where he described his reading style as enjoying books without demanding deep understanding of every detail. This can be positive (appreciating the gestalt) or negative (superficiality). Modern usage often criticizes shallow engagement with material, though it sometimes positively describes a relaxed, enjoyment-focused approach to reading.
chineseExample_tr: 他淺嘗輒止的閱讀讓他不求甚解。

example: The team is preparing intensively for the upcoming competition.
chineseExample: 球队正在厉兵秣马，为即将到来的比赛做准备。
description: This idiom describes sharpening (厉) weapons (兵) and feeding (秣) horses (马). It originated from ancient military preparations where armies would ensure weapons were sharp and horses well-fed before battle. The phrase appeared in Zuozhuan describing state preparations for warfare. Modern usage describes thorough preparation for any significant undertaking, emphasizing the importance of readiness before action.
metaphoric_meaning: Make thorough preparations; get ready for action
description_tr: This idiom describes sharpening (厲) weapons (兵) and feeding (秣) horses (馬). It originated from ancient military preparations where armies would ensure weapons were sharp and horses well-fed before battle. The phrase appeared in Zuozhuan describing state preparations for warfare. Modern usage describes thorough preparation for any significant undertaking, emphasizing the importance of readiness before action.
chineseExample_tr: 球隊正在厲兵秣馬，為即將到來的比賽做準備。

meaning: Make noise in east, strike in west
example: The competitor's marketing campaign was just a diversion from their real strategy.
chineseExample: 竞争对手的营销活动只是声东击西的策略。
description: This idiom describes making noise (声) in the east (东) while striking (击) in the west (西). It is one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems, describing tactical deception by creating diversions. The strategy involves drawing enemy attention to one location while attacking another. Modern usage describes any misdirection tactic in business, negotiations, or competition.
description_tr: This idiom describes making noise (聲) in the east (東) while striking (擊) in the west (西). It is one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems, describing tactical deception by creating diversions. The strategy involves drawing enemy attention to one location while attacking another. Modern usage describes any misdirection tactic in business, negotiations, or competition.
chineseExample_tr: 競爭對手的營銷活動只是聲東擊西的策略。

example: Sometimes stepping back from a negotiation can lead to better results.
chineseExample: 有时在谈判中以退为进反而能取得更好的结果。
description: This idiom describes using (以) retreat (退) as (为) advance (进). It embodies Daoist and strategic wisdom that apparent weakness can become strength. By seeming to yield or withdraw, one may gain advantageous position. The concept appears throughout Chinese military and philosophical texts. Modern usage describes strategic concessions that ultimately lead to greater gains.
description_tr: This idiom describes using (以) retreat (退) as (為) advance (進). It embodies Daoist and strategic wisdom that apparent weakness can become strength. By seeming to yield or withdraw, one may gain advantageous position. The concept appears throughout Chinese military and philosophical texts. Modern usage describes strategic concessions that ultimately lead to greater gains.
chineseExample_tr: 有時在談判中以退為進反而能取得更好的結果。

example: Instead of defending directly, they attacked the competitor's main market.
chineseExample: 他们没有直接防守，而是围魏救赵，攻击对手的主要市场。
description: This idiom refers to besieging (围) Wei (魏) to rescue (救) Zhao (赵). During the Warring States period, when Wei attacked Zhao, strategist Sun Bin advised attacking Wei's capital instead of sending relief forces directly. This forced Wei to withdraw. It became one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems, describing indirect approaches that relieve pressure by attacking the attacker's vulnerabilities.
metaphoric_meaning: Relieve pressure by attacking the source
description_tr: This idiom refers to besieging (圍) Wei (魏) to rescue (救) Zhao (趙). During the Warring States period, when Wei attacked Zhao, strategist Sun Bin advised attacking Wei's capital instead of sending relief forces directly. This forced Wei to withdraw. It became one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems, describing indirect approaches that relieve pressure by attacking the attacker's vulnerabilities.
chineseExample_tr: 他們沒有直接防守，而是圍魏救趙，攻擊對手的主要市場。

meaning: Wishing to capture, first let go
example: The detective pretended to drop the case to make the suspect lower his guard.
chineseExample: 侦探假装放弃案件，欲擒故纵，让嫌疑人放松警惕。
description: This idiom describes wanting (欲) to capture (擒) but intentionally (故) letting go (纵). It is one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems where releasing a target creates false security before the final capture. Zhuge Liang famously used this strategy, capturing and releasing Meng Huo seven times until he surrendered genuinely. Modern usage describes patience in pursuit and the psychology of capture.
metaphoric_meaning: Give slack before tightening; let go to catch
description_tr: This idiom describes wanting (欲) to capture (擒) but intentionally (故) letting go (縱). It is one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems where releasing a target creates false security before the final capture. Zhuge Liang famously used this strategy, capturing and releasing Meng Huo seven times until he surrendered genuinely. Modern usage describes patience in pursuit and the psychology of capture.
chineseExample_tr: 偵探假裝放棄案件，欲擒故縱，讓嫌疑人放鬆警惕。

meaning: Embrace the incomplete, guard the deficient
example: The company's resistance to new technology showed they were clinging to outdated methods.
chineseExample: 公司对新技术的抵制表明他们抱残守缺。
description: This idiom describes embracing (抱) the incomplete (残) and guarding (守) the deficient (缺). Originally from Liu Xiang's writings, it described scholars who stubbornly preserved corrupted or incomplete texts. The phrase criticizes those who cling to outdated or imperfect things while rejecting improvement. Modern usage criticizes conservative attitudes that resist beneficial change.
metaphoric_meaning: Cling to the old and outdated; resist progress
description_tr: This idiom describes embracing (抱) the incomplete (殘) and guarding (守) the deficient (缺). Originally from Liu Xiang's writings, it described scholars who stubbornly preserved corrupted or incomplete texts. The phrase criticizes those who cling to outdated or imperfect things while rejecting improvement. Modern usage criticizes conservative attitudes that resist beneficial change.
chineseExample_tr: 公司對新技術的抵制表明他們抱殘守缺。

meaning: Stick to old ways and restrict oneself
example: The industry leader's complacency allowed competitors to overtake them.
chineseExample: 行业领导者固步自封的态度让竞争对手超越了他们。
description: This idiom describes sticking (固) to one's steps (步) and self (自) restricting (封). It criticizes those who refuse to advance beyond their current position, becoming prisoners of their own comfort zone. The phrase warns against complacency and the dangers of not adapting to change. Modern usage describes individuals or organizations that stop growing due to satisfaction with the status quo.
description_tr: This idiom describes sticking (固) to one's steps (步) and self (自) restricting (封). It criticizes those who refuse to advance beyond their current position, becoming prisoners of their own comfort zone. The phrase warns against complacency and the dangers of not adapting to change. Modern usage describes individuals or organizations that stop growing due to satisfaction with the status quo.
chineseExample_tr: 行業領導者固步自封的態度讓競爭對手超越了他們。

meaning: Fish for fame and angle for reputation
example: His charity work seemed more about publicity than genuine help.
chineseExample: 他的慈善工作似乎更多是沽名钓誉，而非真正的帮助。
description: This idiom describes seeking to buy (沽) fame (名) and fishing (钓) for reputation (誉). It criticizes those who pursue recognition through calculated actions rather than genuine merit. The imagery of fishing suggests patience and deliberate effort in acquiring reputation. Modern usage criticizes those whose good deeds are motivated primarily by desire for recognition rather than altruism.
description_tr: This idiom describes seeking to buy (沽) fame (名) and fishing (釣) for reputation (譽). It criticizes those who pursue recognition through calculated actions rather than genuine merit. The imagery of fishing suggests patience and deliberate effort in acquiring reputation. Modern usage criticizes those whose good deeds are motivated primarily by desire for recognition rather than altruism.
chineseExample_tr: 他的慈善工作似乎更多是沽名釣譽，而非真正的幫助。

meaning: Command with chin gestures and breath
example: The arrogant manager treated employees like servants.
chineseExample: 傲慢的经理颐指气使地对待员工。
description: This idiom describes commanding others with chin (颐) gestures (指) and breathing (气) to direct (使). It depicts someone so arrogant they give orders through mere facial expressions and sighs rather than words. This behavior was associated with tyrannical masters commanding servants. Modern usage criticizes bossy, imperious behavior that treats others as beneath verbal communication.
description_tr: This idiom describes commanding others with chin (頤) gestures (指) and breathing (氣) to direct (使). It depicts someone so arrogant they give orders through mere facial expressions and sighs rather than words. This behavior was associated with tyrannical masters commanding servants. Modern usage criticizes bossy, imperious behavior that treats others as beneath verbal communication.
chineseExample_tr: 傲慢的經理頤指氣使地對待員工。

example: He walked through the office with an insufferably superior attitude.
chineseExample: 他趾高气扬地走过办公室。
description: This idiom describes walking with toes (趾) high (高) and spirits (气) raised (扬). It depicts someone strutting with such arrogance that even their toes seem elevated. The phrase captures the physical embodiment of pride in one's gait and bearing. Modern usage describes people who display obvious superiority complex through their demeanor and body language.
description_tr: This idiom describes walking with toes (趾) high (高) and spirits (氣) raised (揚). It depicts someone strutting with such arrogance that even their toes seem elevated. The phrase captures the physical embodiment of pride in one's gait and bearing. Modern usage describes people who display obvious superiority complex through their demeanor and body language.
chineseExample_tr: 他趾高氣揚地走過辦公室。

example: His overconfidence blinded him to his obvious weaknesses.
chineseExample: 他妄自尊大，看不到自己明显的弱点。
description: This idiom describes unreasonably (妄) considering oneself (自) honorable and great (尊大). It is the opposite of 妄自菲薄, describing excessive self-importance without justification. The phrase appeared in historical texts criticizing rulers who overestimated their power. Modern usage warns against arrogance and inflated self-assessment that can lead to poor judgment and failure.
metaphoric_meaning: Be conceited; have an inflated ego
description_tr: This idiom describes unreasonably (妄) considering oneself (自) honorable and great (尊大). It is the opposite of 妄自菲薄, describing excessive self-importance without justification. The phrase appeared in historical texts criticizing rulers who overestimated their power. Modern usage warns against arrogance and inflated self-assessment that can lead to poor judgment and failure.
chineseExample_tr: 他妄自尊大，看不到自己明顯的弱點。

example: She felt nervous waiting for the interview results.
chineseExample: 等待面试结果时，她忐忑不安。
description: This idiom describes a heart that is agitated (忐忑) and not (不) at peace (安). The characters 忐忑 are onomatopoeic, mimicking the sound and feeling of a heart beating irregularly with anxiety. The phrase captures the physical sensation of nervousness and worry. Modern usage describes anxiety, apprehension, and the restless feeling before uncertain outcomes.
description_tr: This idiom describes a heart that is agitated (忐忑) and not (不) at peace (安). The characters 忐忑 are onomatopoeic, mimicking the sound and feeling of a heart beating irregularly with anxiety. The phrase captures the physical sensation of nervousness and worry. Modern usage describes anxiety, apprehension, and the restless feeling before uncertain outcomes.
chineseExample_tr: 等待面試結果時，她忐忑不安。

example: After returning the lost wallet, he felt completely at ease.
chineseExample: 归还了丢失的钱包后，他心安理得。
description: This idiom describes the heart (心) being at peace (安) because reason (理) is satisfied (得). It describes the inner tranquility that comes from knowing one has acted correctly. The phrase connects moral behavior with psychological well-being. Modern usage describes the peace of mind that follows ethical actions, or sometimes sarcastically, those who feel unjustifiably content with questionable behavior.
metaphoric_meaning: Feel at ease with a clear conscience
description_tr: This idiom describes the heart (心) being at peace (安) because reason (理) is satisfied (得). It describes the inner tranquility that comes from knowing one has acted correctly. The phrase connects moral behavior with psychological well-being. Modern usage describes the peace of mind that follows ethical actions, or sometimes sarcastically, those who feel unjustifiably content with questionable behavior.
chineseExample_tr: 歸還了丟失的錢包後，他心安理得。

meaning: So pleased as to forget one's form
example: His success made him careless about his professional conduct.
chineseExample: 成功让他得意忘形，忽视了职业操守。
description: This idiom describes being so pleased (得意) that one forgets (忘) proper form or behavior (形). It warns that excessive joy or success can lead to loss of composure and judgment. The phrase appeared in texts about the dangers of hubris following achievement. Modern usage warns against letting success go to one's head, leading to inappropriate behavior or poor decisions.
description_tr: This idiom describes being so pleased (得意) that one forgets (忘) proper form or behavior (形). It warns that excessive joy or success can lead to loss of composure and judgment. The phrase appeared in texts about the dangers of hubris following achievement. Modern usage warns against letting success go to one's head, leading to inappropriate behavior or poor decisions.
chineseExample_tr: 成功讓他得意忘形，忽視了職業操守。

example: Working for someone with unpredictable moods is exhausting.
chineseExample: 为一个喜怒无常的人工作令人精疲力竭。
description: This idiom describes having joy (喜) and anger (怒) without (无) consistency (常). It depicts someone whose emotions are unpredictable and volatile. The phrase appeared in historical texts describing capricious rulers whose moods endangered their subjects. Modern usage describes moody, emotionally unstable individuals whose unpredictability creates stress for those around them.
description_tr: This idiom describes having joy (喜) and anger (怒) without (無) consistency (常). It depicts someone whose emotions are unpredictable and volatile. The phrase appeared in historical texts describing capricious rulers whose moods endangered their subjects. Modern usage describes moody, emotionally unstable individuals whose unpredictability creates stress for those around them.
chineseExample_tr: 為一個喜怒無常的人工作令人精疲力竭。

example: Upon discovering the betrayal, he became furious beyond control.
chineseExample: 发现背叛后，他怒不可遏。
description: This idiom describes anger (怒) that cannot (不可) be suppressed (遏). It depicts rage so intense that it overwhelms all attempts at self-control. The phrase appeared in texts describing righteous fury at injustice or betrayal. Modern usage describes intense anger that demands expression, whether justified or not, and the loss of emotional regulation in extreme circumstances.
description_tr: This idiom describes anger (怒) that cannot (不可) be suppressed (遏). It depicts rage so intense that it overwhelms all attempts at self-control. The phrase appeared in texts describing righteous fury at injustice or betrayal. Modern usage describes intense anger that demands expression, whether justified or not, and the loss of emotional regulation in extreme circumstances.
chineseExample_tr: 發現背叛後，他怒不可遏。

example: The children's faces lit up when they saw the presents.
chineseExample: 孩子们看到礼物时喜笑颜开。
description: This idiom describes being so happy (喜) that one smiles (笑) and the face (颜) opens up (开). It captures the physical transformation that genuine joy brings to facial expression. The phrase depicts pure happiness visible in one's countenance. Modern usage describes obvious happiness that shows clearly on someone's face, the kind of joy that cannot be hidden.
description_tr: This idiom describes being so happy (喜) that one smiles (笑) and the face (顏) opens up (開). It captures the physical transformation that genuine joy brings to facial expression. The phrase depicts pure happiness visible in one's countenance. Modern usage describes obvious happiness that shows clearly on someone's face, the kind of joy that cannot be hidden.
chineseExample_tr: 孩子們看到禮物時喜笑顏開。

example: He described his adventure with animated enthusiasm.
chineseExample: 他眉飞色舞地描述着自己的冒险经历。
description: This idiom describes eyebrows (眉) flying (飞) and expression (色) dancing (舞). It captures the animated facial expressions of someone excited or enthusiastic. The vivid imagery of moving eyebrows and dancing features depicts obvious enjoyment. Modern usage describes animated enthusiasm, the visible excitement when someone talks about something they love or find thrilling.
description_tr: This idiom describes eyebrows (眉) flying (飛) and expression (色) dancing (舞). It captures the animated facial expressions of someone excited or enthusiastic. The vivid imagery of moving eyebrows and dancing features depicts obvious enjoyment. Modern usage describes animated enthusiasm, the visible excitement when someone talks about something they love or find thrilling.
chineseExample_tr: 他眉飛色舞地描述著自己的冒險經歷。

example: Receiving the good news, her whole face lit up with happiness.
chineseExample: 收到好消息，她眉开眼笑。
description: This idiom describes brows (眉) opening (开) and eyes (眼) smiling (笑). It depicts the full facial expression of genuine happiness where both brows relax upward and eyes crinkle with joy. The phrase captures authentic emotion visible across the entire face. Modern usage describes obvious happiness, the kind of joy that involves the whole face and cannot be faked.
description_tr: This idiom describes brows (眉) opening (開) and eyes (眼) smiling (笑). It depicts the full facial expression of genuine happiness where both brows relax upward and eyes crinkle with joy. The phrase captures authentic emotion visible across the entire face. Modern usage describes obvious happiness, the kind of joy that involves the whole face and cannot be faked.
chineseExample_tr: 收到好消息，她眉開眼笑。

example: He's been looking gloomy ever since he heard about the budget cuts.
chineseExample: 自从听说预算削减后，他一直愁眉苦脸。
description: This idiom describes worried (愁) brows (眉) and a bitter (苦) face (脸). It depicts the physical manifestation of anxiety and unhappiness in facial expression. The furrowed brow and downturned expression signal distress. Modern usage describes visible worry or displeasure, someone whose face clearly shows they are troubled or unhappy about something.
description_tr: This idiom describes worried (愁) brows (眉) and a bitter (苦) face (臉). It depicts the physical manifestation of anxiety and unhappiness in facial expression. The furrowed brow and downturned expression signal distress. Modern usage describes visible worry or displeasure, someone whose face clearly shows they are troubled or unhappy about something.
chineseExample_tr: 自從聽說預算削減後，他一直愁眉苦臉。

example: She walked into the interview with confidence.
chineseExample: 她昂首挺胸地走进面试室。
description: This idiom describes holding the head (首) high (昂) and the chest (胸) out (挺). It depicts confident, proud posture that shows self-assurance. The phrase captures how confidence manifests in body language. Modern usage describes walking or standing with visible confidence, the posture of someone who believes in themselves and faces challenges boldly.
description_tr: This idiom describes holding the head (首) high (昂) and the chest (胸) out (挺). It depicts confident, proud posture that shows self-assurance. The phrase captures how confidence manifests in body language. Modern usage describes walking or standing with visible confidence, the posture of someone who believes in themselves and faces challenges boldly.
chineseExample_tr: 她昂首挺胸地走進面試室。

example: A good leader welcomes constructive criticism.
chineseExample: 好的领导者闻过则喜，欢迎建设性批评。
description: This idiom describes hearing (闻) faults (过) and then (则) rejoicing (喜). It comes from Mencius praising Yu the Great who welcomed criticism as opportunities for improvement. The phrase embodies the ideal attitude toward feedback. Modern usage praises those who genuinely appreciate criticism, understanding that identifying faults is the first step to self-improvement.
description_tr: This idiom describes hearing (聞) faults (過) and then (則) rejoicing (喜). It comes from Mencius praising Yu the Great who welcomed criticism as opportunities for improvement. The phrase embodies the ideal attitude toward feedback. Modern usage praises those who genuinely appreciate criticism, understanding that identifying faults is the first step to self-improvement.
chineseExample_tr: 好的領導者聞過則喜，歡迎建設性批評。

example: She readily accepts good suggestions from her team.
chineseExample: 她从善如流，乐于接受团队的好建议。
description: This idiom describes following (从) what is good (善) like (如) flowing water (流). It comes from Zuozhuan describing Duke Huan of Jin who accepted good advice as naturally as water flows downhill. The phrase praises adaptability in accepting wisdom. Modern usage describes people who readily adopt good ideas without ego resistance, flowing toward improvement naturally.
description_tr: This idiom describes following (從) what is good (善) like (如) flowing water (流). It comes from Zuozhuan describing Duke Huan of Jin who accepted good advice as naturally as water flows downhill. The phrase praises adaptability in accepting wisdom. Modern usage describes people who readily adopt good ideas without ego resistance, flowing toward improvement naturally.
chineseExample_tr: 她從善如流，樂於接受團隊的好建議。

example: The researcher worked tirelessly on her project for years.
chineseExample: 研究人员孜孜不倦地工作了多年。
description: This idiom describes working diligently (孜孜) without (不) becoming weary (倦). The doubled character 孜 emphasizes continuous effort. The phrase praises sustained dedication that doesn't diminish over time. Modern usage describes tireless workers who maintain their diligence and enthusiasm despite long hours or years of effort, never losing their drive.
description_tr: This idiom describes working diligently (孜孜) without (不) becoming weary (倦). The doubled character 孜 emphasizes continuous effort. The phrase praises sustained dedication that doesn't diminish over time. Modern usage describes tireless workers who maintain their diligence and enthusiasm despite long hours or years of effort, never losing their drive.
chineseExample_tr: 研究人員孜孜不倦地工作了多年。

example: The mentor skillfully guided the students through difficult concepts.
chineseExample: 导师循循善诱，带领学生理解困难的概念。
description: This idiom describes guiding (循循) skillfully (善) and leading (诱). It comes from the Analects where Yan Hui praised Confucius' teaching method of systematic, patient guidance. The doubled 循 emphasizes the gradual, step-by-step approach. Modern usage describes excellent teaching methodology - patient, systematic instruction that leads students to understanding without force.
description_tr: This idiom describes guiding (循循) skillfully (善) and leading (誘). It comes from the Analects where Yan Hui praised Confucius' teaching method of systematic, patient guidance. The doubled 循 emphasizes the gradual, step-by-step approach. Modern usage describes excellent teaching methodology - patient, systematic instruction that leads students to understanding without force.
chineseExample_tr: 導師循循善誘，帶領學生理解困難的概念。

example: Despite explaining it many times, she remained patient with the students.
chineseExample: 尽管解释了很多遍，她仍然诲人不倦。
description: This idiom describes teaching (诲) people (人) without (不) weariness (倦). It comes from the Analects where Confucius described his tireless dedication to education. The phrase embodies the ideal teacher who maintains enthusiasm despite repetition. Modern usage praises patient educators who never tire of helping others learn, maintaining dedication regardless of how many times they must explain.
description_tr: This idiom describes teaching (誨) people (人) without (不) weariness (倦). It comes from the Analects where Confucius described his tireless dedication to education. The phrase embodies the ideal teacher who maintains enthusiasm despite repetition. Modern usage praises patient educators who never tire of helping others learn, maintaining dedication regardless of how many times they must explain.
chineseExample_tr: 儘管解釋了很多遍，她仍然誨人不倦。

example: Good teachers adapt their methods to each student's learning style.
chineseExample: 好老师会因材施教，根据每个学生的学习方式调整方法。
description: This idiom describes teaching (施教) according to (因) ability (材). It embodies Confucius' educational philosophy of tailoring instruction to individual students' talents and needs. The phrase recognizes that different people learn differently. Modern usage praises adaptive teaching methods that recognize individual differences and adjust accordingly for optimal learning outcomes.
description_tr: This idiom describes teaching (施教) according to (因) ability (材). It embodies Confucius' educational philosophy of tailoring instruction to individual students' talents and needs. The phrase recognizes that different people learn differently. Modern usage praises adaptive teaching methods that recognize individual differences and adjust accordingly for optimal learning outcomes.
chineseExample_tr: 好老師會因材施教，根據每個學生的學習方式調整方法。

example: The scholarship program aims to make education accessible to all.
chineseExample: 奖学金项目旨在有教无类，让所有人都能接受教育。
description: This idiom describes having (有) education (教) without (无) categories (类). It comes from the Analects expressing Confucius' principle that education should be available regardless of social class. He accepted students from all backgrounds. Modern usage advocates for equal educational opportunity regardless of wealth, status, or background - education as a universal right.
description_tr: This idiom describes having (有) education (教) without (無) categories (類). It comes from the Analects expressing Confucius' principle that education should be available regardless of social class. He accepted students from all backgrounds. Modern usage advocates for equal educational opportunity regardless of wealth, status, or background - education as a universal right.
chineseExample_tr: 獎學金項目旨在有教無類，讓所有人都能接受教育。

meaning: Teach by words and personal example
example: The best parents teach not just through words but through their actions.
chineseExample: 最好的父母言传身教，不仅用言语更用行动教导孩子。
description: This idiom describes teaching through words (言传) and personal example (身教). It recognizes that instruction involves both verbal teaching and modeling behavior. Actions often teach more powerfully than words. Modern usage emphasizes the importance of practicing what one preaches, recognizing that children and students learn as much from observing behavior as from formal instruction.
description_tr: This idiom describes teaching through words (言傳) and personal example (身教). It recognizes that instruction involves both verbal teaching and modeling behavior. Actions often teach more powerfully than words. Modern usage emphasizes the importance of practicing what one preaches, recognizing that children and students learn as much from observing behavior as from formal instruction.
chineseExample_tr: 最好的父母言傳身教，不僅用言語更用行動教導孩子。

meaning: Peaches and plums fill the gate
example: The retired professor has trained hundreds of successful scholars.
chineseExample: 退休教授桃李满门，培养了数百名成功的学者。
description: This idiom describes peaches (桃) and plums (李) filling (满) the gate (门). Students are metaphorically called peach and plum trees because teachers cultivate them like gardeners. A teacher with many successful students has peaches and plums everywhere. Modern usage describes successful educators whose former students have achieved great things.
description_tr: This idiom describes peaches (桃) and plums (李) filling (滿) the gate (門). Students are metaphorically called peach and plum trees because teachers cultivate them like gardeners. A teacher with many successful students has peaches and plums everywhere. Modern usage describes successful educators whose former students have achieved great things.
chineseExample_tr: 退休教授桃李滿門，培養了數百名成功的學者。

example: She returned from vacation looking refreshed and happy.
chineseExample: 她度假回来春风满面。
description: This idiom describes spring wind (春风) filling (满) the face (面). It depicts someone whose face radiates warmth and happiness like the pleasant spring breeze. The phrase captures the appearance of contentment and good fortune. Modern usage describes someone who looks happy and satisfied, whose good mood or good fortune is visible in their pleasant expression.
description_tr: This idiom describes spring wind (春風) filling (滿) the face (面). It depicts someone whose face radiates warmth and happiness like the pleasant spring breeze. The phrase captures the appearance of contentment and good fortune. Modern usage describes someone who looks happy and satisfied, whose good mood or good fortune is visible in their pleasant expression.
chineseExample_tr: 她度假回來春風滿面。

example: The army maintained strict discipline, not disturbing the civilians.
chineseExample: 军队纪律严明，对百姓秋毫无犯。
description: This idiom describes not (无) violating (犯) even the finest autumn hair (秋毫). Autumn down refers to the tiny new hairs animals grow for winter - extremely fine. The phrase describes perfect discipline where not even the smallest thing is taken or disturbed. Modern usage describes scrupulous honesty and discipline, strict adherence to rules that doesn't allow even minor violations.
metaphoric_meaning: Show strict discipline; not take anything
description_tr: This idiom describes not (無) violating (犯) even the finest autumn hair (秋毫). Autumn down refers to the tiny new hairs animals grow for winter - extremely fine. The phrase describes perfect discipline where not even the smallest thing is taken or disturbed. Modern usage describes scrupulous honesty and discipline, strict adherence to rules that doesn't allow even minor violations.
chineseExample_tr: 軍隊紀律嚴明，對百姓秋毫無犯。

example: He lived his life with nothing to hide or regret.
chineseExample: 他活得坦坦荡荡，无愧于心。
description: This idiom describes being open (坦坦) and broad (荡荡). The doubled characters emphasize the degree of openness and freedom from anxiety. It depicts someone who has nothing to hide and faces life with clear conscience. Modern usage describes people who live honestly and openly, whose transparency allows them to face anything without guilt or fear.
description_tr: This idiom describes being open (坦坦) and broad (蕩蕩). The doubled characters emphasize the degree of openness and freedom from anxiety. It depicts someone who has nothing to hide and faces life with clear conscience. Modern usage describes people who live honestly and openly, whose transparency allows them to face anything without guilt or fear.
chineseExample_tr: 他活得坦坦蕩蕩，無愧於心。

example: She is genuine - the same person in public and private.
chineseExample: 她表里如一，在公众场合和私下是同一个人。
description: This idiom describes the outside (表) and inside (里) being as (如) one (一). It depicts consistency between one's public persona and private self, between words and actions. The phrase values authenticity and integrity. Modern usage praises those who are genuine - the same person regardless of audience or circumstance, without pretense or duplicity.
description_tr: This idiom describes the outside (表) and inside (裡) being as (如) one (一). It depicts consistency between one's public persona and private self, between words and actions. The phrase values authenticity and integrity. Modern usage praises those who are genuine - the same person regardless of audience or circumstance, without pretense or duplicity.
chineseExample_tr: 她表裡如一，在公眾場合和私下是同一個人。

example: He kept his promise because he believed a man's word is his bond.
chineseExample: 他信守承诺，因为他相信一言既出，驷马难追。
description: This idiom describes once (既) a word (一言) is spoken (出). It is often paired with 驷马难追 (four horses cannot catch it) to emphasize that spoken words cannot be taken back.
